 Constitutional        the delicate tension between protecting minority rights and          pluralism. It ensures that minority
 Balance               ensuring state oversight.                                            communities are not penalised (by losing
                     y The SC clarified that while minorities have the right to             their constitutional rights) simply because
                       administer, the State can still impose regulatory measures to        they seek formal state recognition or
                       ensure academic excellence and prevent maladministration.            university status.

                                                            Article 32
It empowers every citizen to directly approach the Supreme Court of India if they believetheir fundamental rights have been violated
by the government or any other authority. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called it the "heart and soul of the Constitution."
It makes FRs real and effectively justiciable by providing a mechanism for their enforcement.
The Supreme Court has the power to issue Writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo-Warranto) for the
enforcement of FRs.

                                              The Five Prerogative Writs

       Type of Writ                                                         Application
 Habeas Corpus                 y Inquires into the legality of a person's detention. The court orders immediate release if the
 ("To Have the Body")            detention is found to be unlawful.
                               y Locus standi is the legal principle that determines whether a person has the right to approach
                                 a court, requiring a sufficient interest or direct injury in the matter.
                               y Exception: In Habeas Corpus petitions, the rule of locus standi is relaxed- any person acting
                                 in good faith (such as a relative, friend, or even a public-spirited individual) may file the
                                 petition on behalf of the detained person
 Mandamus                       Commands a public official, lower court, or public body to perform a specific public duty that
 ("We Command")                 they have failed or refused to perform.
 Prohibition                    Issued by a higher court to prevent a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction or
 ("To Forbid")                  violating natural justice. It acts as an order to stop ongoing proceedings.
 Certiorari                     Issued to a lower court or tribunal after a final decision has been made. It quashes the lower body's
 ("To Be Certified")            order if there is a legal error on the record, lack of jurisdiction, or violation of natural justice.
 Quo Warranto                   Challenges a person's authority to hold a public office to prevent illegal usurpation. It can be
 ("By What Authority")          filed by any interested person, even if not directly affected.

 Exceptions to       y Article 31A: Protects five categories of laws (mostly related to agricultural land reforms and industry
 Fundamental           management) from being challenged on the grounds of contravention of Articles 14 and 19.
 Rights              y Article 31B (Ninth Schedule): Protects acts and regulations included in the Ninth Schedule from being
                       challenged on the grounds of contravention of any of the fundamental rights. (Though constrained
                       post-I.R. Coelho case, 2007).
                     y Article 31C: Protects laws made to implement Directive Principles under Article 39(b) and (c) from
                       being challenged on the grounds of Articles 14 and 19.
 Article 33          y Empowers Parliament to restrict or abrogate the FRs of members of armed forces, paramilitary forces,
                       police forces, and intelligence agencies to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and the
                       maintenance of discipline.
 Article 34          y Provides for the restriction of FRs while martial law (military rule) is in force in any area within the
                       territory of India.


                         Electoral Bonds Case: Article 19(1)(a) vs. Article 21
              Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR) v. Union of India (2024)

                                              Right to Property in India
The Right to Property in India transitioned from a heavily litigated Fundamental Right to a standard legal right under the 44th
Amendment Act of 1978. Below is a summary of its historical and current constitutional status.

        Aspect                                                          Provisions
 Original Position        A Fundamental Right under Part III; Article 19(1)(f) guaranteed the right to acquire/hold property, and
                          Article 31protected against deprivation without public purpose and compensation.
 Early Amendments         Parliament enacted several amendments (1st, 4th, 25th, etc.) adding Articles 31A, 31B, and 31C to
                          protect land reform laws from judicial review and expedite welfare projects.
 Kesavananda Bharati      The Supreme Court upheld Parliament’s power to amend property-related Fundamental Rights, while
 Case (1973)              introducing the Basic Structure doctrine.
 44th Amendment Act       The turning point: The 44th Amendment removed the Right to Property from Part III and inserted Article
 (1978)                   300A in Part XII.
 Current Status           It is now a legal/constitutional right under Article 300A ("no person shall be deprived of his property
 (Post-1978)              except by authority of law"). Property can now be deprived only through valid legal authority, and both
                          executive and legislative actions remain subject to constitutional scrutiny.
 Impact on Land           The government now has greater flexibility to acquire land for infrastructure and social welfare
 Acquisition              without the strict hurdles of Fundamental Right litigation.


 Exceptions                  The State must still pay compensation when acquiring: 1) Property of minority educational
 (Compensation               institutions (Art. 30), or 2) Land under personal cultivation within statutory ceiling limits (Art.
 Guaranteed)                 31A).
 Recent Supreme              Kolkata Municipal Corporation & Anr. v. Bimal Kumar Shah & Ors. (May 2024): The Court
 Court Stance                reaffirmed protections under Article 300A, stressing that the rule of law mandates due process and
                             timely, fair compensation when individuals are deprived of property.

                                   The Three Pillars of Privacy
                    (Puttaswamy Framework) - Paternity and Bodily autonomy

   Dimension of Privacy                   Constitutional Application                        Relevance/ Medical Testing
 Physical / Bodily Autonomy          The right to control one's own body and           Force drawing of blood or amniotic fluid
                                     protect it from non-consensual state or           directly violates a woman’s physical
                                     private intrusion.                                autonomy.
 Informational Privacy               The right to control the dissemination of         DNA contains the ultimate biological
                                     personal, medical, and biological data.           blueprint. Forced extraction is a breach of
                                                                                       informational privacy.
 Decisional Autonomy                 The freedom to make intimate personal             A woman's choice to carry a pregnancy
                                     choices regarding marriage, procreation,          without being subjected to adversarial legal
                                     and family                                        interrogations.

                                    WHY PRE-NATAL DNA TESTING IS DENIED?
The Autonomy Defense: A pre-natal DNA test requires invasive procedures on the mother's body. Under the Puttaswamy doctrine,
no court can force a woman to undergo an invasive medical procedure solely to satisfy a husband's suspicion of infidelity, as it
constitutes a gross violation of her bodily integrity.

The Protection of Legitimacy Defense: The courts act as parens patriae (parent of the nation) for the unborn child. Ordering a
paternity test risks removing the legal legitimacy of the child, causing lifelong social stigma. The child's right to identity and dignity
(Article 21) outweighs the father's right to truth.

The Evidentiary Defense: A DNA test is not meant to be a shortcut for divorce proceedings. If a husband claims adultery, he must
prove it through independent evidence, not by forcing the State (Courts) to violate his wife's biological privacy.

                      The Constitutional Trilemma: Health vs. Trade vs. Religion
      The Competing                      The Constitutional                                  The Constitutional
          Right                             Argument                                            Argument
 Article 25                        Pro-cracker advocates argued that       The Health Caveat: Article 25 is explicitly subject to
 (Freedom of Religion)             bursting fireworks during Diwali        "public order, morality, and health". Furthermore,
                                   is a core religious custom              bursting crackers is a modern addition and fails the
                                   protected under freedom of              "Essential Religious Practices" (ERP) Test. Thus, it
                                   practice.                               enjoys no absolute protection.
 Article 19(1)(g)                  Firecracker manufacturers in            The Survival Caveat: The SC ruled that Article 19(6)
 (Right to Trade/Business)         Sivakasi argued a ban destroys          allows reasonable restrictions in the public interest. The
                                   their fundamental right to a            economic livelihood of a few cannot be placed above
                                   livelihood and creates mass             the biological survival and respiratory health of
                                   unemployment.                           millions.
 Article 21                        Citizens (especially children and       The Ultimate Victor: The Court applied the
 (Right to Life/Clean Air)         the elderly) argued that the toxic      Precautionary Principle, having generally prioritised
                                   smog violates their right to live       public health and environmental concerns under Article
                                   with human dignity and health.          21 while balancing competing rights under Articles 19
                                                                           and 25.


Madras HC Ruling (2025): Balancing Free Movement, Protest, and Public Order

     Theme               Constitutional /                                        Madras High Court
                       Statutory Provision
 Nature of           Article 19(1)(a) – Freedom      y The Court held that the rights to move freely, assemble peacefully, and
 Fundamental         of Speech and Expression          express grievances are integral to democratic participation.
 Rights              Article 19(1)(b) – Right to     y However, these rights are not absolute and remain subject to reasonable
                     Assemble Peacefully               restrictions in the interest of public order under Article 19(2) & 19(3).
                     Article 19(1)(d) – Freedom
                     of Movement
 Rights vs.          Police regulations requiring    y The Court observed that where the law requires prior permission for
 State's             prior permission for public       protests, participants must comply with the conditions imposed.
 Regulatory          demonstrations                  y Fundamental rights cannot be exercised in disregard of a valid regulatory
 Power                                                 framework.
 Methods of          Public order principles          The Court held that methods such as climbing cellphone towers, life-
 Protest &           under Article 19(2) &            threatening fasts involving senior citizens, and displaying human skulls and
 Public Order        19(3)                            bones are inconsistent with the constitutional guarantee of peaceful protest.
 Check on            Sections 55, 56 & 156,          y The Court held that railway authorities may deboard passengers only in
 Executive           Railways Act, 1989                limited circumstances - such as travelling without a ticket, suffering
 Arbitrariness                                         from an infectious disease, or occupying an unauthorised
                                                       compartment.
                                                     y A passenger with a valid ticket cannot be removed merely because he
                                                       intends to protest. Any such action would be unlawful and officials
                                                       concerned may be held accountable.
Fundamental Rights are not merely static constraints on the State; they are a dynamic framework that ensures the dignified existence
of citizens. The Supreme Court's expansive interpretation, especially of Article 21, coupled with the Basic Structure Doctrine, ensures
that the Fundamental Rights continue to evolve and adapt to modern challenges (e.g., cyber laws, privacy, and environmental
protection), keeping the constitutional machinery vibrant and citizen-centric.
pyq: Explain the constitutional perspectives of Gender Justice with the help of relevant Constitutional Provisions and case laws. (2023)
Right of movement and residence throughout the territory of India are freely available to the Indian citizens, but these rights are not absolute.” Comment. (2022)
Examine the scope of Fundamental Rights in the light of the latest judgement of the Supreme Court on Right to Privacy. (2017)

Does the right to clean environment entail legal regulation on burning crackers during Diwali? Discuss in the light of Article 21 of Indian Constitution and judgements of the apex in this regard. (2015)

What do you understand about the concept “freedom of speech and expression”? Does it cover hate speech also? Why do the films in India stand on a slightly different plane from other forms of expression? Discuss. (2014)

chapters.part: Provisions and Basic Structure
chapters.text:
6             Directive Principles of State
                        Policy
Enshrined in Part IV of the Constitution (Articles 36 to 51). Borrowed from the Irish Constitution. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar described
them as the "novel features" of the Constitution. Granville Austin called DPSP and Fundamental Rights the "Conscience of the
Constitution". While Fundamental Rights establish political democracy, DPSPs aim to establish socio-economic democracy. They
signify the transition from a colonial 'Police State' to a modern 'Welfare State'.
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    Discuss the possible factors that inhibit India from enacting for its citizens a uniform
           civil code as provided for in the Directive Principles of State Policy. (2015)


6.1 Dimensions of DPSP
 Nature           y Non-Justiciable: They are not legally enforceable by the courts for their violation. The State cannot be
 (Article 37)       sued for failing to implement them.
                  y Fundamental in Governance: Article 37 explicitly declares that it shall be the duty of the State to apply
                    these principles in making laws.
                       ♦ They act as an "Instrument of Instructions" for all successive governments, regardless of their
                          political ideology.
                  y Judicial Compass: Though non-justiciable, the Supreme Court uses them to determine the constitutional
                    validity of laws. Courts often interpret laws implementing DPSPs sympathetically while balancing them
                    with Fundamental Rights.
 Socialistic      y Article 38: Promote the welfare of people by securing a social order permeated by justice- social,
 Principles         economic, and political and minimise inequalities in income, status, and opportunities.
 (Egalitarian     y Article 39: Securing adequate livelihood, equitable distribution of material resources of the community
 Society)           [39(b)], prevention of concentration of wealth [39(c)], equal pay for equal work for men and women.
                       ♦ Example: Abolition of the Zamindari system, Nationalization of Banks (1969), and enactment of the
                         Equal Remuneration Act, 1976.
                  y Article 39A: Equal justice and free legal aid to the poor.
                       ♦ Example: Enactment of the Legal Services Authorities Act (1987) and the establishment of Lok
                         Adalats.
                  y Article 41 & 42: Right to work, education, public assistance (old age, sickness), and provision for just/
                    humane conditions of work and maternity relief.
                  y Articles 43 & 43A: Securing a living wage, decent standard of life, and participation of workers in the
                    management of industries.
 Gandhian         y Article 40: Organise Village Panchayats and endow them with powers of self-government. (Implemented
 Principles         via 73rd Amendment).
                  y Articles 43 & 43B: Promote cottage industries on an individual/cooperative basis in rural areas, and
                    promote voluntary formation of co-operative societies.
                       ♦ Example: Establishment of Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC), Coir Board, Silk
                         Board, etc.
                  y Article 46: Promote educational and economic interests of SCs, STs, and other weaker sections, protecting
                    them from social injustice.
                  y Article 47: Prohibit the consumption of intoxicating drinks and drugs which are injurious to health.
                  y Article 48: Prohibit the slaughter of cows, calves, and other milch/draught cattle to improve their breeds.
 Liberal-         y Article 44: Secure for all citizens a Uniform Civil Code (UCC) throughout the country.
 Intellectual          ♦ The Supreme Court has repeatedly reminded the state to implement it (Shah Bano case, Sarla
 Principles               Mudgal case). The debate centers on balancing gender justice/national integration with minority
                          cultural autonomy. The recent Uttarakhand UCC act is a major case study.
                  y Article 45: Provide early childhood care and education (ECCE) for all children until they complete the age
                    of six years.
                  y Articles 48 & 48A: Organise agriculture/animal husbandry on modern, scientific lines, and protect/
                    improve the environment, forests, and wildlife.
                  y Article 49: Protect monuments, places, and objects of artistic or historic interest (national importance).
                  y Article 50: Separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.
                  y Article 51: Promote international peace and security, maintain just relations between nations, and foster
                    respect for international law.


 Evolution via     y 42nd Amendment (1976 - "Mini Constitution"): Added 39A (free legal aid), 39(f) (healthy development
 Amendments          of children), 43A (workers in management), and 48A (environment).
                   y 44th Amendment (1978): Added Article 38(2) requiring the State to minimise inequalities in income,
                     status, facilities, and opportunities.
                   y 86th Amendment (2002): Changed the subject matter of Article 45. Made elementary education a
                     Fundamental Right (Article 21A) and directed the State to provide early childhood care under Article 45.
                   y 97th Amendment (2011): Added Article 43B relating to co-operative societies.

                                               Uniform Civil Code (UCC)
 UCC: It is a proposed framework of laws designed to replace religion-based personal laws - which govern marriage, divorce,
 inheritance, and adoption - with a single, common set of laws that applies equally to all Indian citizens regardless of their religion,
 caste, or gender.
 Landmark Supreme Court Verdicts
 The judiciary has frequently highlighted the need for a UCC:
  Shah Bano Case (1985): The Supreme Court emphasised the desirability of Article 44 implementation.
  Sarla Mudgal Case (1995): Addressed the issue of bigamy and the conflict between personal laws on matters of marriage.
  Shayara Bano vs. Union of India Case (2017): Challenged the practice of Triple Talaq and declared it unconstitutional.

 Need and Significance of UCC
  Strengthening Secularism: A UCC aligns with India's secular principles by establishing a common set of laws for all
     citizens, irrespective of religion.
  Equality for All: It would eliminate discriminatory practices based on religion in personal laws, guaranteeing equal rights.
  Promoting Gender Equity: By overriding existing religious personal laws, a UCC could remove gender-based biases,
     leading to a more just society.
  Protecting Vulnerable Groups: It could offer stronger safeguards for the rights of women and religious minorities who
     might be disadvantaged by current personal laws.
  Freedom of Choice: It would provide greater freedom for individuals to choose their partners without religious or caste
     restrictions under civil law principles
 Recent Developments & State Implementations
  Uttarakhand: Became the first Indian state post-independence to implement a UCC. It covers marriage, divorce, adoption,
     and inheritance with a strong emphasis on gender equality. It bans polygamy and child marriage, and notably mandates the
     registration of live-in relationships to safeguard partner and child rights. (Note: Goa has had a UCC since 1867).
  Gujarat: Has followed suit by forming a committee to draft a UCC, indicating growing momentum at the state level.
  2018 Law Commission Recommendation: The 21st Law Commission previously recommended codifying existing personal
     laws rather than implementing a complete UCC, suggesting that the focus should be on universal principles of fairness and
     equality.
                           Challenges                                                             Way Forward
   Religious Interference: May be perceived as an                          Introduce the UCC gradually in stages (piecemeal
   infringement on religious freedom, restricting personal                 approach) to minimize disruption and allow for smoother
   matters.                                                                adaptation
   Minority Apprehensions: Minorities may fear the imposition              Prioritize equity and equal treatment for all citizens during the
   of majority-driven codes and the erosion of cultural traditions         codification process.
   Threat to Diversity: Enforcing uniformity could                         Foster public debate and open discussions to ensure
   undermine India's rich cultural and religious pluralism.                transparency and address concerns.
   Drafting Difficulties: Creating a universally acceptable code is        Include legal and social experts from diverse backgrounds in
   complex due to diverse ethnic and regional traditions                   the drafting process
   Constitutional Conflict: Potential friction between Article             Launch educational campaigns and engage with different
   14 (Equality) and Article 25 (Freedom of Religion)                      communities to build awareness and garner support

6.2 Constitutional Tussle: FRs vs. DPSPs
The Evolution:
1. Champakam Dorairajan Case (1951): The Supreme Court ruled that in case of a conflict, FRs would prevail. DPSPs have
    to run as a subsidiary to FRs.
2. Golaknath Case (1967): The SC held that FRs cannot be amended for the implementation of DPSPs.
3. The Parliament's Pushback (24th & 25th Amendments): Parliament inserted Article 31C, stating that laws made to implement
    Article 39(b) and (c) (equitable distribution of resources) cannot be struck down even if they violate Articles 14 and 19.


4. Kesavananda Bharati Case (1973): The SC upheld the validity of the first provision of Article 31C, cementing the primacy
   of 39(b) and (c) over Articles 14 and 19.
5. 42nd Amendment Act (1976): The government attempted to give legal primacy and supremacy to all DPSPs over Articles
   14 and 19.
6. Minerva Mills Case (1980) - The Final Settlement: The SC struck down the 42nd Amendment's extension. It ruled that the
   Indian Constitution is founded on the "bedrock of the balance between Part III (FRs) and Part IV (DPSPs)".
"To give absolute primacy to one over the other is to disturb the harmony of the Constitution. This harmony and balance is an essential
feature of the Basic Structure." While Fundamental Rights act as injunctions against State tyranny, the Directive Principles are positive
mandates for State action.Together, they form the core of transformative constitutionalism, ensuring that political liberty is not a
hollow promise, but is firmly anchored in the reality of social equity and economic justice.
pyq: Discuss the possible factors that inhibit India from enacting for its citizens a uniform civil code as provided for in the Directive Principles of State Policy. (2015)

chapters.part: Provisions and Basic Structure
chapters.text:
7             Fundamental Duties
The original Constitution (1950) contained only Fundamental Rights, not Duties. The framers assumed that citizens would naturally
perform their duties. Rights and duties are correlative and inseparable. As Mahatma Gandhi famously said, "The true source of right
is duty. If we all discharge our duties, rights will not be far to seek." Borrowed from the Constitution of the erstwhile USSR.

7.1 Dimensions of FDs
 Evolution and        y The Swaran Singh Committee (1976): Formed during the Internal Emergency to make
 Nature                 recommendations about fundamental duties.
                           ♦ The committee argued that citizens must realise that in addition to enjoying rights, they also have
                              certain obligations.
                      y 42nd Amendment Act (1976): Inserted a new part, Part IVA, containing only one article: Article
                        51A. It initially codified 10 Fundamental Duties.
                      y 86th Amendment Act (2002): Added the 11th duty regarding the provision of educational
                        opportunities for children.
                      y Restricted to Citizens: Unlike some Fundamental Rights (e.g., Articles 14 or 21), which apply to
                        foreigners, FDs are strictly confined to Indian citizens.
                      y Non-Justiciable: Similar to DPSPs, they are not directly enforceable by courts. There is no
                        constitutional provision for direct legal sanction for their violation.
 Duties               y Civic & Patriotic Duties:
                          ♦ To abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals, the National Flag, and the National Anthem
                              [51A(a)].
                          ♦ To uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India [51A(c)].
                          ♦ To defend the country and render national service when called upon [51A(d)].
                      y Moral & Social Duties:
                          ♦ To cherish and follow the noble ideals that inspired the national struggle for freedom [51A(b)].
                          ♦ To promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood; to renounce practices derogatory to
                              the dignity of women [51A(e)].
                          ♦ To value and preserve the rich heritage of the country's composite culture [51A(f)].
                      y Environmental Duties:
                          ♦ To protect and improve the natural environment (forests, lakes, rivers, wildlife) and have compassion
                              for living creatures [51A(g)]. (Directly linked to DPSP Article 48A and FR Article 21).
                      y Intellectual & Progressive Duties:
                          ♦ To develop scientific temper, humanism, and the spirit of inquiry and reform [51A(h)].
                          ♦ To strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity [51A(j)].
                      y Public Property & Parental Duties:
                          ♦ To safeguard public property and abjure violence [51A(i)].
                          ♦ To provide opportunities for education to one's child or ward between the ages of 6 and 14 years
                              [51A(k) - Added in 2002].
 Landmark             y AIIMS Students’ Union v. AIIMS (2001): The Supreme Court declared that Fundamental Duties are
 Judicial               as important as Fundamental Rights. Though Part IVA is not enforceable by writs; it provides a
 Pronouncements         valuable guide to constitutional interpretation.
                      y M.C. Mehta Case: The SC has heavily relied on Article 51A(g) (environmental duty) to order the
                        closure of polluting industries, mandating that the state and citizens must jointly protect the
                        environment.
                      y Shyam Narayan Chouksey v. Union of India (2016): The "National Anthem Case". The SC initially
                        mandated playing the anthem in cinema halls to instil a sense of committed patriotism (Article 51A(a)),
                        though it later made it optional.
                      y Ranganath Mishra Case (2003): The SC directed the Government of India to take steps to
                        operationalise the recommendations of the Justice JS Verma Committee regarding the dissemination
                        and enforcement of Fundamental Duties (Article 51-A) among citizens.


7.2 Statutory backing for Fundamental Duties
1. Upholding the Sovereignty, Integrity, and National Symbols
        Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971: Directly enforces the duty to respect the Constitution, the
         National Flag, and the National Anthem by penalising any disrespect or insult toward them.
        Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA), 1967: Strongly aligns with the duty to uphold and protect the sovereignty,
         unity, and integrity of India by strictly prosecuting unlawful and terror-related activities that threaten the nation.
2. Promoting Brotherhood and Social Harmony
        The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955: Reflects the duty to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood
         by strictly prohibiting untouchability and punishing discrimination based on religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth.
3. Safeguarding the Dignity of Women
        Women-Centric Legislations: The duty to renounce practices derogatory to the dignity of women is legally reinforced
         by statutes like the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961, and the Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act (formerly SITA, 1956).
4. Protecting and Improving the Environment
        Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972: Reinforces the citizen's duty to have compassion for living creatures by protecting
         rare and endangered species and criminalising illegal poaching and wildlife trade.
        Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980: Safeguards natural habitats and checks indiscriminate deforestation, directly echoing
         the constitutional duty to protect and improve the natural environment, including forests, lakes, and rivers.
5. Safeguarding Public Property and Abjuring Violence
        Prevention of Damage to Public Property Act, 1984: Directly translates the moral duty of safeguarding public property
         and abjuring violence into a legal mandate by penalizing acts of vandalism and destruction.
        Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023: Several penal sections within the newly enacted BNS regarding theft, mischief,
         and vandalism indirectly enforce the duty to respect the rights of others and protect public/state property.
While Fundamental Rights act as a shield against State tyranny, Fundamental Duties serve as a moral compass for the citizenry. In
an era marked by environmental degradation, communal polarisation, and digital misinformation, Article 51A transitions democracy
from a mere political mechanism into a participatory civic responsibility. As the Supreme Court has noted, a careful balancing of
Part III (Rights) and Part IVA (Duties) is essential to realize the vibrant, progressive republic envisioned in the Preamble.

chapters.part: Provisions and Basic Structure
chapters.text:
8                Constitutional Amendments
                          and Article 368
Part XX, Article 368 grants Parliament the constituent power to amend the Constitution and lays down the procedure. The framers
wanted a Constitution that could adapt to changing times but wasn't so fragile that ruling parties could alter its core for political gains.
The Indian Constitution strikes a unique balance between extreme rigidity (like the USA, requiring a special convention) and extreme
flexibility (like the UK, amended by ordinary law).

                                                    UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     The Indian Constitution has conferred the amending power on the ordinary legislative
             institutions with a few procedural hurdles. In view of this statement, examine the
             procedural and substantive limitations on the amending power of Parliament to change
             the Constitution. (2025)
      2.     Explain the significance of the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act. To what extent
             does it reflect the accommodative spirit of federalism. (2023)
      3.     Explain the salient features of the Constitution (One Hundred and First Amendment)
             Act, 2016. Do you think it is efficacious enough ‘to remove cascading effect of taxes and
             provide for common national market for goods and services’? (2017)


8.1 Procedure and Classification
      Aspect                                                                Details
 Procedure for          y Initiation: A Constitutional Amendment bill can only be introduced in Parliament (Lok Sabha or
 Amendment                Rajya Sabha). State legislatures cannot initiate an amendment.
 (Article 368)          y Introduction: Can be introduced by a Minister or a Private Member. No prior permission of the
                          President is required.
                        y Majority Required: The bill must be passed in each House by a Special Majority (a majority of the
                          total membership of the House and a majority of two-thirds of the members of the House present and
                          voting).
                        y No Joint Sitting: Each House must pass the bill separately. If there is a deadlock, there is no
                          provision for a joint sitting(unlike ordinary bills).
                        y The Ratification Clause: If the bill seeks to amend federal provisions of the Constitution, it must also
                          be ratified by the legislatures of half of the states by a simple majority.
                        y Presidential Assent: After passage (and ratification, if needed), it goes to the President. The President
                          must give assent. President cannot withhold it or return it for reconsideration (made obligatory by the

                        y Amendments by Special Majority (Under Article 368)

                             ♦ All other provisions not covered in the first and third categories.
                        y Amendments by Special Majority + Ratification by Half the States
                             ♦ Provisions related to the federal structure (centre-state relations) require this rigid process.
                             ♦ Examples: Election of the President and its manner.
                             ♦ Extent of the executive power of the Union and states.
                             ♦ Supreme Court and High Courts.
                             ♦ Distribution of legislative powers (Union, State, and Concurrent lists).
                             ♦ Goods and Services Tax Council (Article 279A).
                             ♦ Article 368 itself.

Article 368 ensures that the Indian Constitution is neither a rigid monolith that breaks under the pressure of changing societal needs
nor a malleable tool vulnerable to majoritarian whims. The Supreme Court's 'Basic Structure Doctrine' serves as the ultimate safety
valve, ensuring that while the Constitution remains a 'living document' capable of reflecting progressive realities (like GST or Women's
Reservation), its democratic, secular, and egalitarian soul remains immortal.

pyq: The Indian Constitution has conferred the amending power on the ordinary legislative institutions with a few procedural hurdles. In view of this statement, examine the procedural and substantive limitations on the amending power of Parliament to change the Constitution. (2025)
Explain the significance of the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act. To what extent does it reflect the accommodative spirit of federalism. (2023)

chapters.t: Parliamentary vs Presidential form of Government
chapters.part: Provisions and Basic Structure
chapters.text:
9              Parliamentary vs Presidential
                         form of Government
The Constitution of India provides for a parliamentary form of government both at the Centre and in the states. Articles 74 and 75
deal with the parliamentary system at the Union level, while Articles 163 and 164 contain provisions for the states. The bedrock of
the Indian political setup is the principle of collective responsibility of the Executive (Council of Ministers) to the popular house of
the Legislature (Lok Sabha/Vidhan Sabha), ensuring a 'responsible government' over a merely 'stable' one.

9.1 Comparison of Parliamentary System and Presidential System
    Feature           Parliamentary System (Indian Context)                          Presidential System (e.g., USA)
 Executive           Dual Executive: The President is the nominal             Single Executive: The President is both the Head of
 Structure           executive (de jure head), while the Prime Minister       State and the Head of Government.
                     is the real executive (de facto head).
 Separation of       Fusion of Powers: The executive is drawn from            Strict Separation: The executive and legislature are
 Powers              the legislature. Ministers must be Members of            completely separate. The President's cabinet
                     Parliament (or become one within 6 months).              comprises non-legislators.
 Accountability      High Accountability: The Council of Ministers is         Low Accountability: The executive is not
                     collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha (Article       responsible to the legislature. They cannot be
                     75). They remain in power only as long as they           removed by a simple vote of no-confidence.
                     enjoy majority support.
 Tenure              Uncertain: The government can fall at any time if        Fixed: The President and legislature are elected for
                     it loses the majority (via a no-confidence motion).      a fixed term and can only be removed via a rigorous,
                                                                              quasi-judicial impeachment process.
 Dissolution of      The Prime Minister can advise the President to           The President cannot dissolve the lower house
 Lower House         dissolve the Lok Sabha before the expiry of its          (e.g., the US House of Representatives).
                     term to seek fresh elections.
 Merits              y Harmony between Legislature & Executive:               y Stable Government: The fixed tenure ensures the
                       Reduces deadlocks as the executive is a part of          government can execute its vision without the
                       the legislature and commands a majority.                 constant threat of a no-confidence motion.
                     y Prevents Despotism: Power is dispersed among           y Definiteness in Policies: Stability allows for the
                       the Council of Ministers, and their survival             formulation and execution of long-term
                       depends on parliamentary scrutiny, preventing a          developmental policies.
                       dictatorship.                                          y Government by Experts: The President can
                     y Ready Alternative Government: If the ruling              more easily appoint domain experts (technocrats)
                       party loses its majority, the President can invite       to lead ministries without worrying about their
                       the opposition to form the government without            political electability.
                       imposing immediate elections.
                     y Wide Representation: Allows representation of
                       diverse regions, castes, and communities in the
                       executive, which is crucial for India's pluralistic
                       society.
 Demerits            y Unstable Government: Prone to collapse due to          y Conflict between Organs: Frequent deadlocks
                       defections or coalition withdrawals (e.g.,               between the President and the Legislature,
                       frequent government collapses in the 1990s in            especially if they are controlled by rival political
                       India).                                                  parties.
                     y No Continuity of Policies: Frequent changes in         y Non-Responsible: The executive is not dependent
                       government may lead to policy paralysis or               on legislative confidence for survival.
                       reversal of previous policies.                         y Autocratic Tendencies: Concentrating massive
                     y Executive dominance over legislature: If a               power in the hands of a single individual carries
                       party enjoys an absolute majority, the cabinet           the risk of authoritarianism.
                       can bypass rigorous legislative scrutiny (e.g.,
                       during the 1975 Emergency).


 9.2 Why Did India Adopt the Parliamentary System?
 During the Constituent Assembly debates, a Presidential system was proposed (notably by Prof. K.T. Shah), but the founding
 fathers chose the Parliamentary system for four primary reasons:
 1. Familiarity with the System: India had grown accustomed to the parliamentary model introduced incrementally during
    British rule (e.g., the Government of India Acts of 1919 and 1935). K.M. Munshi argued that it would be unwise to discard
    a system functioning for decades.
 2. Preference for Responsibility over Stability: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar explicitly stated that a democratic executive must satisfy
    two conditions: stability and responsibility. Since no system can guarantee both equally, the Assembly preferred daily and
    periodic accountability (via questions, debates, and elections) over the absolute stability of a Presidential system.
 3. Need to Avoid Legislative-Executive Conflicts: An infant democracy struggling with partition, poverty, and integration
    could not afford the frequent deadlocks characteristic of the American system. A unified approach to nation-building was
    required.
 4. Nature of Indian Society: India’s extreme diversity (religious, linguistic, ethnic) required a system that could accommodate
    varied groups within the core power structure (the Council of Ministers), which a single Presidential executive might fail
    to provide.
 9.3 Should India Switch to a Presidential System?
 Arguments in Favour of a Switch:
     It would ensure political stability and end the culture of "horse-trading," defections, and coalition blackmail.
     It would allow the direct entry of technocrats and experts into governance instead of relying solely on career politicians.
     Relatively, it would force voters to choose a national leader based on a unified national agenda, rather than fragmented
      regional, caste, or religion-based voting.
 Arguments Against a Switch (The Prevailing View):
     Basic Structure Doctrine: The Supreme Court (in the Kesavananda Bharati case, 1973) held that the Parliamentary system
      is a part of the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution. Therefore, Parliament cannot amend it into a Presidential system.
     Swaran Singh Committee (1976): Formed to review the Constitution, this committee concluded that the parliamentary
      system has been doing well and there is no need to replace it.
     Risk of Centralisation: Given India's socio-economic vulnerabilities and vast inequalities, concentrating executive power
      in one individual could lead to authoritarianism and alienate marginalised regions.
While the parliamentary system in India has suffered from flaws like the criminalisation of politics, defections, and occasional
instability, the solution lies in reforming the electoral process and strengthening intra-party democracy, rather than abandoning the
system altogether. The Parliamentary framework, enshrined in Articles 74 and 75, with its inherent mechanism of daily accountability,
remains best suited to accommodate the vast and pluralistic ethos of the Indian Republic.
subs: 9.1 Comparison of Parliamentary System and Presidential System
9.2 Why Did India Adopt the Parliamentary System?
9.3 Should India Switch to a Presidential System?

chapters.part: Provisions and Basic Structure
chapters.text:
10                  Emergency Provisions
                         and Impacts
The Emergency provisions are enshrined in Part XVIII (Articles 352 to 360) of the Indian Constitution. Their primary rationale is
to safeguard the sovereignty, unity, integrity, and security of the country, as well as the democratic political system. As Dr. B.R.
Ambedkar noted in the Constituent Assembly, the Indian Constitution is unique because it can be "both unitary as well as federal
according to the requirements of time and circumstances." During an emergency, the federal structure seamlessly converts into
a unitary one without requiring a formal constitutional amendment.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Account for the legal and political factors responsible for the reduced frequency of using
            Article 356 by the Union Governments since mid-1990s. (2023)
      2.    Under what circumstances can the Financial Emergency be proclaimed by the President
            of India? What consequences follow when such a declaration remains in force? (2018)

10.3 Judicial Review & Landmark Judgments
The Judiciary has played a monumental role in checking the arbitrary use of Emergency provisions, especially Article 356.
 Minerva Mills Case (1980): The Supreme Court held that the proclamation of a National Emergency (Article 352) is not
    immune from judicial review. It can be struck down if it is proven to be malafide, absurd, or based on extraneous facts.
 S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994): The absolute bedrock judgement for Article 356.
          It held that Article 356 is subject to judicial review.
          The state assembly should only be dissolved after Parliamentary approval.
          A floor test in the assembly is the only definitive test of a majority, not the Governor's subjective assessment.
          Secularism is a "Basic Feature"; dismissing a state government for anti-secular acts is constitutionally valid.
 Nabam Rebia Case (2016 - Arunachal Pradesh) & Harish Rawat Case (2016 - Uttarakhand): The Supreme Court heavily
    came down on the Centre for the arbitrary imposition of Article 356, physically restoring the ousted state governments and
    ordered floor test restoration.
          The Court ruled that the Governor's discretionary powers under Article 163 are limited and subject to judicial review.
 Anuradha Bhasin Case (2020): While dealing with the communication lockdown in J&K, the SC ruled that indefinite
    suspensions (a quasi-emergency measure) are impermissible. Any restriction on fundamental rights must pass the Test of
    Proportionality.

Test of Proportionality
The Test of Proportionality is a highly significant legal doctrine used by courts to determine whether a government's restriction on
a citizen's Fundamental Rights is justified.

In simple terms, it means the government cannot use a "sledgehammer to crack a nut." The restriction placed on a right must be
proportionate to the goal the government is trying to achieve.


In India, the Supreme Court formally adopted this test in the landmark K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) case (the Right
to Privacy judgment) and extensively applied it in the Anuradha Bhasin (2020) case regarding internet shutdowns in Jammu &
Kashmir.
Whenever a government action curtails a fundamental right (like Freedom of Speech, Movement, or Privacy), the courts test that
action using a four-pronged framework.
To pass the test and be considered constitutional, the government's action must satisfy all four of the following conditions:

The 4 Prongs of the Proportionality Test
1. Legality (Action backed by Law) The restriction cannot be arbitrary or based on mere executive orders. It must be expressly
   backed by a valid, enacted law.
          Example: You cannot restrict internet access just because a police officer wants to; it must be done under a specific
           law like the Telecom Suspension Rules.
2. Legitimate State Aim (Need) The law must seek to achieve a legitimate public or state interest.
          Example: Preventing riots, protecting national security, or stopping the spread of a pandemic are legitimate aims.
3. Rational Connection (Suitability) There must be a logical link between the measure taken and the objective it aims to achieve.
   The restriction must actually help in solving the problem.
          Example: If the goal is to catch a thief in a neighborhood, imposing a nationwide curfew has no rational connection
           to the goal.
4. Necessity (Least Restrictive Alternative) This is the most critical step. The government must prove that the measure they
   chose is the least intrusive way to achieve the goal. If there is an alternative method that achieves the same result while restricting
   rights less, the government must choose that alternative.
          Example: If stopping a specific violent website is the goal, the government should block just that website (less restrictive)
           rather than shutting down the entire internet for the whole state (highly restrictive).

10.4 Way Forward: Commission Recommendations
   Sarkaria Commission (1988): Emphasised that Article 356 must be used sparingly and strictly as a "measure of last resort"
    only when all other constitutional avenues (like issuing warnings under Article 355) have been exhausted
   Punchhi Commission (2010): Localised Emergency: Recommended a constitutional framework for a "Localized Emergency"
    under Article 355. Instead of dismissing the entire state government and dissolving the assembly under Article 352 or 356, the
    Centre should be able to take control of only the specific troubled district/area to tackle the crisis locally → never constitutionally
    implemented
          Codification of Guidelines: Suggested that the guidelines laid down in the S.R. Bommai judgement should be explicitly
           incorporated into the text of the Constitution to remove any lingering ambiguities.
The Emergency provisions are a necessary "safety valve" to protect the Indian Republic from existential threats. However, the
transition from the era of frequent President's Rule (1970s-80s) to the current restrained approach demonstrates the maturation of
Indian democracy. Strengthened by the 44th Amendment and the vigilant oversight of the Supreme Court (Bommai and Nabam
Rebia cases), these provisions now function closer to Dr. Ambedkar's original vision- a dormant weapon strictly reserved for the
ultimate defense of the Constitution, not a political tool for federal subversion.


Comparison Of The
Indian Constitutional
 Scheme With That
Of Other Countries

chapters.part: Comparison of the Indian constitutional scheme with that of other countries
chapters.text:
11                 Comparisons with the USA, UK,
                        France etc.
As Dr. B.R. Ambedkar famously remarked, the Indian Constitution was drafted after 'ransacking all the known Constitutions
of the world'. However, it is not a blind plagiarism of Western models but a brilliant eclectic synthesis. It ingeniously blends
the Parliamentary responsibility of the UK with the Judicial Supremacy of the USA, tailored specifically to manage the colossal
diversity of the Indian subcontinent. India also borrowed from Ireland, Canada, Australia, Germany, South Africa, etc., to
reinforce the “synthesis” idea.

11.1 India v/s United Kingdom (The Westminster Model)
India adopted the British Parliamentary system but completely rejected its foundational premise: the absolute sovereignty
of Parliament.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Compare and contrast the British and Indian approaches to Parliamentary
            sovereignty. (2023)
      2.    The judicial systems in India and the UK seem to be converging as well as diverging
            in recent times. Highlight the key points of convergence and divergence between the
            two nations in terms of their judicial practices. (2020)

Comparative constitutional analysis highlights that while India has borrowed several institutional features from other democracies,
it has adapted them to its unique historical, social and federal realities. This selective adaptation has enabled the Constitution to
balance democratic governance, social justice, national unity and constitutional accountability, making it a distinct and resilient
constitutional model.

chapters.text:
12                 Centre-State relations
                        (Legislative, Administrative and Financial)
The Indian Constitution provides for a federal structure with a strong unitary bias. Granville Austin described it as "Cooperative
Federalism," while K.C. Wheare called it "Quasi-Federal." The Constitution regulates Centre-State relations across three
domains: Legislative (Part XI), Administrative (Part XI), and Financial (Part XII).
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    To what extent is Article 370 of the Indian Constitution, bearing marginal note
            “Temporary provision with respect to the State of Jammu and Kashmir”, temporary?
            Discuss the future prospects of this provision in the context of Indian polity. (2016)
      2.    Recent directives from the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas are perceived by the
            ‘Nagas’ as a threat to override the exceptional status enjoyed by the State. Discuss in
            light of Article 371A of the Indian Constitution (2013)
      3.    Though the federal principle is dominant in our Constitution and that principle is one
            of its basic features, but it is equally true that federalism under the Indian Constitution
            leans in favour of a strong Centre, a feature that militates against the concept of strong
            federalism. Discuss. (2014)


12.1 Introduction to Indian Federalism
Federalism is a system of government where power is structurally divided between a central authority and constituent units (states/
provinces). In India, Article 1 of the Constitution describes India as a "Union of States." Crucially, the word "Federation" is
nowhere mentioned in the Constitution. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar justified the term "Union" to signify two things:
1. The Indian Federation is not the result of an agreement among independent states (unlike the USA).
2. The states have no right to secede from the federation.

India represents a "Holding Together" federation, where a large, diverse nation consciously divides its power between the Centre
and States to accommodate administrative convenience and regional diversity, as opposed to a "Coming Together" federation (like
the USA) where independent states pooled their sovereignty.

Types/Models of Federalism
1.   Cooperative Federalism
2.   Competitive Federalism
3.   Asymmetric Federalism
4.   Fiscal Federalism

                                              Asymmetric Federalism
    Concept: A system where all constituent states do not possess identical powers. The Constitution accommodates unique
     geographic, ethnic, or historical challenges by granting special status to certain regions.
    Enshrined in Part XXI of the Constitution ("Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions"), Articles 371 to 371-J embody
     this principle, granting special provisions to 12 states (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Nagaland, Assam, Manipur, Andhra Pradesh,
     Telangana, Sikkim, Mizoram, Arunachal Pradesh, Goa, and Karnataka).

       Type                                     Characteristics & Constitutional Examples
 Vertical             y The Union is indestructible; states are not.
 Asymmetry            y States have no role in ratifying most constitutional amendments (only those affecting federal structure
 (Centre vs.            require 50% state ratification).
 States)              y Existence of Union Territories (UTs) directly governed by the Centre.


Horizontal            y Rajya Sabha Representation: Unequal, based on population (e.g., UP has 31 seats; Sikkim has 1).
Asymmetry             y Article 371 (A-J): Special provisions for specific states (e.g., Naga customary laws in 371A; special
(State vs. State)       development boards for Vidarbha/Saurashtra).
                      y 5th & 6th Schedules: Special administrative setups for tribal areas. The 6th Schedule creates
                        Autonomous District Councils (e.g., in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram).
Among Union           y Delhi & Puducherry: Have legislative assemblies and Chief Ministers.
Territories           y J&K: UT with a legislature (currently under transition).
                      y Other UTs (e.g., Lakshadweep, Andaman): No legislature; ruled purely by central Administrators/Lt.
                        Governors.
                            Why Did India Adopt Asymmetric Federalism?
   Protection of Cultural & Ethnic Identity: Recognising that the tribal customary laws and land-holding systems of the
    Northeast are unique and pre-date the Indian Constitution (e.g., Nagaland and Mizoram).
   Addressing Regional Imbalances (Developmental Asymmetry): To prevent the neglect of historically backward regions
    within a developed state by creating special development boards and ensuring equitable allocation of funds (e.g., Vidarbha in
    Maharashtra, Saurashtra in Gujarat, and Kalyana in Karnataka).
   Smooth Political Integration & Law & Order: To manage the transition of newly integrated regions into the Indian Union
    (e.g., Sikkim) and to handle sensitive border states where the Governor is given special responsibilities for law and order (e.g.,
    Arunachal Pradesh).
                  Cooperative vs. Competitive vs. Confrontational Federalism

12.2 Features of Indian Federalism
                Federal Features                                                   Unitary Features
           (Promotes State Autonomy)                                           (Promotes Strong Centre)
 Dual Polity: Clear existence of Union and State              Single Constitution & Citizenship: States do not have their own
 governments.                                                 constitutions or citizenship (unlike the USA).
 Written Constitution & Supremacy: Limits the                 Emergency Provisions: Converts the federal structure into a unitary

 Division of Powers: The 7th Schedule divides subjects        Overriding Legislative Power: The Centre has residuary powers

 under Article 131).
Indian federalism is a unique "holding together" federation that balances state autonomy with a strong Centre to safeguard national
unity and administrative efficiency. Its flexible design has enabled India to accommodate vast regional diversity while preserving
constitutional stability and cooperative governance.

               The Constitutional and Legal Framework for Fiscal Consolidation
 Fiscal consolidation refers to the policies undertaken by governments to reduce their deficits and accumulation of debt stock.
  FRBM Act, 2003: Acts as the statutory anchor for deficit reduction. The current glide path targets a Union fiscal deficit of
      4.5% by 2025-26 and 3.5% by 2030-31.
  Article 293(3) of the Constitution: Mandates that states must obtain the Union's consent to borrow if they have outstanding
      central loans.
             The 16th FC strictly enforces this to cap State fiscal deficits at 3% of GSDP.
 Curbing Off-Budget Borrowings: The 16th FC mandates integrating all hidden liabilities into state budgets. The CAG is tasked
 with standardised annual reporting of these liabilities, closing loopholes that states use to bypass FRBM limits.

12.4 GST Council and Fiscal Federalism
The GST Council is a joint constitutional forum of the Centre and the States, established to govern the Goods and Services Tax (GST)
- a destination-based indirect tax aimed at creating a ‘One Nation, One Tax’ system.
Constitutional Mandate: Inserted by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016, under Article 279A.
Objective: To pool the fiscal sovereignty of the Centre and States, ensuring a harmonised indirect tax structure across the country.


GST Council
Enshrined under Article 279A via the 101st Constitutional Amendment, the GST Council is India's first truly federal decision-
making institution. It represents a historic leap of faith into 'pooled sovereignty', where the Union and the States voluntarily
surrendered their exclusive taxation domains to forge a unified national market. However, the Council currently navigates a fragile
equilibrium, caught between the constitutional ideal of cooperative federal consensus and the centralising realities of fiscal
majoritarianism.

    Dimension                             Details                                                 Impact
 Basic Structure &      y Chairperson: Union Finance Minister.           y Institutionalises "cooperative federalism" by forcing

                          majority.                                        the Union Executive."

                          Octroi, etc.) to join the GST regime.            post-pandemic stress.

                          about 55% of total GST collections.              black box", severely impacting fund-starved states.

                                                                         legitimate share of national revenues.


Challenges
         Challenge                                                         Description
 The Loss of State Fiscal        Before GST, states could raise VAT or excise duties to fund emergency relief (e.g., floods,
 Autonomy                        pandemics) or fulfil local welfare manifestos. Today, they cannot alter tax rates without the
                                 Council's approval, leaving them financially paralysed during crises.

                                 online gaming), bypassing genuine consensus.

Judicial Interventions & The Transition to "GST 2.0"

                        (similar to the European Union model).                         discussions.

 (Rate Bands)           component within a narrow, pre-defined band                    needed to raise emergency funds without
                        (e.g., +/- 2%).                                                breaking the national macro-tax structure.

 Resolution             Supreme Court judge.                                           veto of the Council.

Way Forward
     Reform Area                                                        Description
 Operationalising the       Immediately finalise the formation of the GST Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) with predominantly
 Independent                judicial (rather than technical) members to resolve Centre-State revenue disputes outside of the
 Tribunal                   highly politicised Council meetings.
 Democratizing the          To reduce the perception of a Central dictatorship, experts recommend amending the weightage to
 Voting Structure           require the affirmative vote of at least 50% of the States (14 states) alongside the Centre's vote for
                            any decision to pass, preventing a few large states and the Centre from bullying smaller states.
 State-Specific             Introduce a Constitutional provision allowing states to levy a temporary micro-cess (e.g., 1%) on
 Emergency Levers           GST within their own borders strictly for notified natural disasters, restoring a sliver of fiscal
                            autonomy without breaking the national supply chain.
 Rate Rationalization       The Council must transition from the complex multi-slab structure (5%, 12%, 18%, 28%) to a
 & Exemptions               simplified three-slab structure (as recommended by various economic committees) to boost
                            compliance, reduce classification disputes, and increase the overall revenue pie for states.
The GST Council was envisioned as the apex temple of cooperative federalism, built on the premise of mutual trust and pooled
sovereignty. However, the Supreme Court’s Mohit Minerals judgement serves as a stark reminder that the Council’s survival depends
not on constitutional coercion but on political statesmanship.

Indian federalism is an evolving, asymmetric arrangement tailored to the nation's unique socio-political realities. While a
"strong Centre" was historically necessary to ensure national integration, the maturation of Indian democracy demands that
states be treated as equal partners. Shifting from a paradigm of "subordination" to one of genuine "cooperation and collaboration"
is essential for India's inclusive growth and the realisation of its constitutional ethos.

pyq: To what extent is Article 370 of the Indian Constitution, bearing marginal note “Temporary provision with respect to the State of Jammu and Kashmir”, temporary? Discuss the future prospects of this provision in the context of Indian polity. (2016)

Recent directives from the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas are perceived by the ‘Nagas’ as a threat to override the exceptional status enjoyed by the State. Discuss in light of Article 371A of the Indian Constitution (2013)

Though the federal principle is dominant in our Constitution and that principle is one of its basic features, but it is equally true that federalism under the Indian Constitution leans in favour of a strong Centre, a feature that militates against the concept of strong federalism. Discuss. (2014)

chapters.part: Comparison of the Indian constitutional scheme with that of other countries
chapters.text:
13                  Inter-State
                         Water Disputes
Inter-state water disputes in India arise when two or more states contest the sharing and use of river waters flowing across political
boundaries. Driven by uneven rainfall, growing agricultural demand, and climate change, these disputes pose a major challenge to
cooperative federalism and sustainable resource management.
Although water is primarily a State subject under the Constitution, the Union is empowered to regulate inter-state rivers in the national
interest. To address such conflicts, Article 262 provides a special constitutional mechanism for adjudication, leading to the enactment
of the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956. However, delays, ad-hoc tribunals, and procedural inefficiencies continue to weaken
the effectiveness of the existing framework.
                                                   UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Constitutional mechanisms to resolve the inter-state water disputes have failed to
            address and solve the problems. Is the failure due to structural or process inadequacy
            or both? Discuss. (2013)


13.1 Constitutional and Legal Framework
    Entry 17, State List: Water - supply, irrigation, canals, drainage, embankments, water storage, water power - is primarily a
     state subject.
    Entry 56, Union List: Gives the Union power to regulate and develop inter-state rivers and river valleys, but only to the extent
     Parliament declares this "expedient in the public interest."
    Article 262(1): Parliament may by law provide for adjudication of any dispute relating to the use, distribution, or control of
     inter-state river waters.
    Article 262(2): Parliament may bar the Supreme Court and all other courts from exercising jurisdiction over such disputes - an
     unusual ouster clause, without direct parallel elsewhere in the Constitution.

13.1.2 Legislation enacted under these provisions:
    River Boards Act, 1956 - allows the constitution of advisory River Boards for integrated basin development. Never
     operationalised, no River Board has ever actually been constituted in nearly seven decades.
            However, some river boards, commissions, authorities have been constituted through government resolution, agreements,
             executive orders or Acts. Example: Andhra pradesh state reorganisation Act, 2014 established - Godavari River
             management boards and Krishna river management board.
    Inter-State River Water Disputes (ISRWD) Act, 1956 - the primary operative law. A state government requests the Centre
     to refer a dispute; if negotiation fails, the Centre must constitute a Water Disputes Tribunal. A published tribunal award has
     the same force as a Supreme Court decree.
    2002 Amendment (Sarkaria Commission recommendations): Mandated a 1-year deadline to constitute a tribunal and a
     3-year deadline (extendable by 2 years) for it to give a decision - deadlines routinely breached in practice.

13.1.3 Inter-State River Water Disputes (ISRWD) Act, 1956

    Section(s)            Provision                                              Key Features
 Sec. 3 & 4           Initiation &           y State may request Centre to refer a dispute. If negotiations fail, Centre must
                      Constitution of          constitute a Tribunal within 1 year (2002 Amendment).
                      Tribunal               y Tribunal comprises Chairperson + 2 judicial members nominated by the CJI.
                                             y Limitation: No mandatory technical/hydrological experts.
 Sec. 5 & 5A          Adjudication &         y Tribunal investigates disputes and submits its award within 3 years (extendable by
                      Vacancies                2 years).
                                             y Clarification references to be decided within 1 year.
                                             y Vacancies are filled by CJI nominees without restarting proceedings.


 Sec. 6 & 6A         Binding Nature &       y Award is published in the Official Gazette, becomes final and binding, and has the
                     Implementation           same force as a Supreme Court decree.
                                            y Centre may establish implementation authorities (e.g., CWMA & CWRC) through
                                              notified schemes.
 Sec. 7–9A           Powers &               y Prohibits discriminatory water rates; avoids overlap with the River Boards Act;
                     Administrative         y Tribunal has Civil Court powers (summons, documents, surveys, data
                     Framework                requisition).
                                            y Centre must maintain a river basin data bank, though data-sharing remains weak
                                              in practice.
 Sec. 11             Bar of Jurisdiction    y Bars jurisdiction of courts over water disputes under Art. 262(2).
                                            y However, the Supreme Court has exercised appellate jurisdiction under Article
                                              136 (SLP), making the exclusion not absolute.
 Sec. 12–14          Miscellaneous &        y Sections 12–13 contain procedural provisions.
                     Special Tribunal       y Section 14 provides for the Ravi-Beas Waters Tribunal under the Rajiv–Longowal
                                              Accord (1985), with relevant provisions of the Act applying mutatis mutandis.

13.2 Issues and Explanation
      Core Issue                                               Explanation & Examples
 Ad hoc-ism of             Separate tribunals are created for every dispute, leading to a massive loss of institutional memory.
 Tribunals                 Once an award is given, the tribunal dissolves.
 Lack of                   There is no universally accepted central repository of water data. States often present conflicting data
 Hydrological Data         on river flow and distress years, breeding mistrust.
 Inordinate Time           The Godavari tribunal took nearly 10 years, and the Mahadayi (Goa-Karnataka-Maharashtra) tribunal
 Lags                      took over 16 years. Justice delayed exacerbates the conflict.
 Non-Compliance by        y Tribunal awards lack a strong enforcement mechanism.
 States                   y Example: In the Sutlej-Yamuna Link (SYL) Canal Dispute, despite Supreme Court directives
                            since 2002 to finish the canal, Punjab refuses to comply, citing over-exploited groundwater,
                            declining river flows, and changed hydrological realities.
                          y In May 2025, the Supreme Court had to step in again, directing Punjab and Haryana to cooperate
                            with the Centre for an "amicable mediation".
 Bypassing                 Though Art 262 bars SC jurisdiction, states routinely bypass this by filing Special Leave Petitions
 Article 262               (Article 136) in the Supreme Court (e.g., Cauvery dispute), defeating the purpose of specialised
                           tribunals.

13.3 Way Forward
To fix these archaic issues, the Inter-State River Water Disputes (Amendment) Bill of 2019 (which was lapsed later) proposed
revolutionary changes
 1. Dispute Resolution Committee (DRC): A mandatory pre-litigation negotiation phase. A dispute will only go to a tribunal if
     the DRC fails to resolve it amicably within 1 year.
 2. Single Permanent Tribunal: Dissolving all existing ad-hoc tribunals to create one permanent tribunal with multiple benches,
     saving costs and retaining technical expertise.
 3. Strict Timelines: Reducing the total adjudication time to a maximum of 4.5 years.
 4. National Data Bank: Mandating the Centre to maintain a transparent, national-level data bank for every river basin to eliminate
     "data wars" between states.
 5. Binding Decision: The decision of the bench will automatically become final and binding, removing the requirement for the
     Centre to officially publish it in the gazette to give it force.
 6. Operationalise the River Boards Act so basin-level cooperative planning has a genuine institutional home, rather than
     remaining entirely dormant.
Inter-state river disputes highlight the tension between federal autonomy and national resource management. A transparent, science-
based, and cooperative institutional framework- supported by reliable hydrological data, time-bound adjudication, and effective
enforcement - is essential to transform water from a source of conflict into an instrument of cooperative federalism.
pyq: Constitutional mechanisms to resolve the inter-state water disputes have failed to address and solve the problems. Is the failure due to structural or process inadequacy or both? Discuss. (2013)

14                  5th and 6th Schedules

Part X of the Indian Constitution details the special administrative frameworks for "Scheduled Areas" and "Tribal Areas"
under Article 244. The framers recognised that tribal populations, due to their distinct socio-cultural practices and historical
disadvantages, required specialised protection to prevent exploitation and land alienation. Consequently, the Constitution
deployed Asymmetric Federalism, establishing the 5th Schedule for mainland tribal areas and the 6th Schedule for the uniquely
autonomous tribes of the North-East.

      Dimension               Fifth Schedule (Article 244(1))               Sixth Schedule (Article 244(2) & 275(1))
