                        Members can become UPSC                 other government jobs.
                        Chairman or SPSC Chairman.

29.1 Indian Civil Services

Constitutional Framework
    Part XIV of the Constitution (Articles 308 to 323): This part deals comprehensively with the services under the Union and
     the States.
    Article 309: Empowers the Parliament and State legislatures to regulate the recruitment and conditions of service of persons
     appointed to public services under the Centre and States, respectively.
    Article 310 (Doctrine of Pleasure): Civil servants hold office during the pleasure of the President (for Union services) or the
     Governor (for State services). This allows the government to terminate the employment of public servants at will.
            Doctrine of Pleasure operates subject to constitutional safeguards under Article 311.
    Article 311: Provides constitutional safeguards to civil servants. It protects them against arbitrary dismissal, removal, or
     reduction in rank without a proper inquiry and an opportunity to be heard.
       Theme                                                              Details
 All India Services      Creation: Parliament can create new AIS, but it strictly requires a Rajya Sabha resolution (2/3rd
 (AIS)                   majority).
 (Article 312)           Evolution: ICS & IP were replaced by IAS & IPS in 1947. The Indian Forest Service (IFS) was added
                         in 1966.
                         Control: Officers serve both Centre and States. Immediate control lies with the State, while ultimate
                         control lies with the Centre.
                         Rationale: Maintains high administrative standards and systemic uniformity and facilitates Centre-
                         State cooperation.


 Accountability,          CVC: Investigates corruption complaints against AIS and Group A Central officers.
 Transparency &           RTI Exemptions: Officials can legally withhold information citing the AIS Conduct Rules (1968) and
 Redressal                Official Secrets Act (1923).
                          Tribunals (Art 323A): Administrative Tribunals (established 1985) ensure speedy dispute resolution
                          for civil servants regarding recruitment and service conditions.
 Proposed Reform:         Constitutional Backing: The 42nd Amendment (1976) empowered Parliament to create an AIJS under
 All India Judicial       Article 312. Not yet created.
 Service (AIJS)           Condition: Cannot include any post inferior to that of a district judge.
                          Need: Aims to resolve high vacancies and varying standards caused by state-wise recruitment by
                          creating a dedicated, standardised professional judicial cadre.

29.2 Challenges and Reforms
   Challenge                             Description                                           Way Forward
 Examination          The Issue: Evolving methods of fraud and                Digital Integrity & Verification: Leverage AI and
 Integrity &          administrative hurdles in verifying caste/income        Blockchain technologies to instantly verify the
 Certificate          claims undermine equitable access.                      authenticity of EWS/OBC/Disability certificates,
 Fraud                Example: The Puja Khedkar Case (2024),                  aligning the process with the Digital India mandate
                      involving allegations of fake OBC and disability        to eliminate forged documents.
                      certificates to gain reservation benefits and extra
                      attempts.
 Opaque               The Issue: Aspirants criticise the lack of early        Examination Modernisation: Transition the CSE
 Evaluation &         answer key disclosure and opaque moderation             to assess 21st-century analytical skills over rote
 Antiquated           techniques.                                             memorisation.
 Cycles               Judicial Stance: In UPSC vs. Angesh Kumar               Baswan Committee (2016): Urgently implement
                      (2018), the SC backed the UPSC, ruling that             logistical improvements to reduce the gruelling
                      disclosing keys before final results would              exam cycle duration and address high absenteeism.
                      compromise exam confidentiality.
 Lateral Entry        The Issue: Recruiting specialists directly at Joint     Advisory Empowerment & Transparency:
 & Bypassing          Secretary/Director levels bypasses the rigorous         Reduce the exemptions given to the Executive
 the Mandate          UPSC CSE process, raising severe concerns about         under the UPSC (Exemption from Consultation)
                      transparency, vetting, and the bypassing of             Regulations. The government must provide
                      constitutionally mandated SC/ST/OBC reservation         transparent, written justifications to Parliament
                      rosters.                                                whenever it recruits outside the standard framework
                                                                              or rejects the Commission's advice.
 The                  The Issue: Despite reservations, structural             Enhancing Inclusivity: Actively boost access by
 Vernacular &         inequalities (like poor translation quality in papers   dramatically improving translation quality in
 Urban-Rural          and the high cost of urban coaching) systemically       question papers and providing state-sponsored,
 Divide               disadvantage rural and vernacular-medium                high-quality coaching to level the playing field
                      aspirants.                                              against affluent urban candidates.
The Public Service Commissions are not merely administrative recruitment agencies; they are the Constitutional custodians of equal
opportunity and administrative neutrality. To effectively meet the governance challenges of a rising digital India, the UPSC and
SPSCs must evolve beyond their traditional paradigms - embracing technological verification to eliminate fraud, ensuring radical
transparency in evaluation, and fiercely defending the sanctity of merit against political and systemic subversions.

chapters.part: Constitutional Bodies
chapters.text:
30                 Comptroller and Auditor
                        General
Described by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar as the most important officer under the Constitution of India, the CAG serves as the supreme
guardian of the public purse. While the Election Commission secures the democratic mandate, the CAG secures democratic
accountability - ensuring that the Executive remains financially subservient to the Legislature, thereby preventing the arbitrary
aggrandisement of public resources.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    “The duty of the Comptroller and Auditor General is not merely to ensure the legality of
            expenditure but also its propriety.” Comment. (2024)
      2.    “The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) has a very vital role to play.” Explain how
            this is reflected in the method and terms of his appointment as well as the range of
            powers he can exercise. (2018)
      3.    Exercise of CAG’s powers in relation to the accounts of the Union and the States is derived
            from Article 149 of the Indian Constitution. Discuss whether audit of the Government’s
            policy implementation could amount to overstepping its own (CAG) jurisdiction (2016)


30.1 Framework
   Constitutional                        Description                                         Significance
     Provision
 Article 148               Appointed by the President. Removed           Provides iron-clad security of tenure (6 years or until
 (Appointment &            only in the same manner as a Supreme          attaining 65 years of age). To prevent post-retirement
 Independence)             Court Judge.                                  bias, the CAG is ineligible for further government
                                                                         office after stepping down.
 Article 149               Performs duties prescribed by Parliament.     The Scope: Audits all expenditures from the
 (Duties & Powers)         This led to the CAG (Duties, Powers and       Consolidated Fund, Contingency Fund, and Public
                           Conditions of Service) Act, 1971.             Accounts of both the Union and the States.
 Article 150               The accounts of the Union and the States      Ensures standardisation and prevents states/
 (Form of Accounts)        shall be kept in such form as the President   departments from hiding fiscal deficits through
                           may, on the advice of the CAG, prescribe.     creative accounting.
 Article 151               Submits audit reports to the President (for   The PAC Nexus: The CAG's reports act as the
 (Audit Reports)           Parliament) and to the Governor (for State    foundational document for the Public Accounts
                           Legislatures).                                Committee (PAC). The CAG acts as a "friend,
                                                                         philosopher, and guide" to the PAC.


30.2 Challenges
   Challenge                                                       Description
 The "Post-         y Unlike the UK's CAG, India's CAG is merely an "Auditor General," not a true "Comptroller." Money
 Mortem"              can be drawn from the Consolidated Fund without the CAG's approval.
 Limitation         y The audit happens ex-post facto (after the money is already spent), meaning the CAG conducts a
                      "post-mortem" of financial disasters rather than preventing them.
 Performance        y The Friction: Beyond checking receipts (Compliance Audit), the CAG conducts Performance /
 Audit vs. Policy     Propriety Audits to check the wisdom and economy of government spending.
 Overreach          y The Controversy: During the 2-G Spectrum and Coalgate reports, the Executive accused the CAG of
                      overstepping into policy-making (estimating "presumptive losses").
                    y SC Stance: In Arvind Gupta vs. UoI, the Supreme Court upheld the CAG's authority to conduct
                      performance audits to ensure public resources are not squandered.
 The PPP and        y With the state transitioning from a "provider" to an "enabler", massive public funds are routed through
 NGO Blindspot        Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs), Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs), and NGOs.
                    y Private entities fiercely resist CAG audits, creating massive blind spots in national financial scrutiny
                      (e.g., disputes over auditing DISCOMs).
 Delay & Denial     y The CAG relies on government departments to provide files. Departments routinely delay or deny
 of Information       access to crucial documents (e.g., defense procurements), crippling the audit process, as the CAG has
                      no punitive power to compel document production.

30.3 Way Forward
  Reform Area                                                  Recommendation
 The Appointment     y Currently, the CAG is appointed solely by the Executive, which violates the principle that the auditor
 Collegium             should not be chosen by the auditee.
                     y Reform: Implement a Collegium system (PM, Chief Justice, and Leader of Opposition) for CAG
                       appointments to ensure absolute neutrality.
 Amending the        y Amend the CAG (Duties, Powers and Conditions of Service) Act to grant the CAG statutory punitive
 DPC Act (1971)        powers to fine or penalise government departments that fail to produce required documents within a
                       stipulated timeframe.
 Bringing PPPs       y Explicitly amend the DPC Act to bring all Public-Private Partnerships, SPVs, and government-funded
 under the Ambit       NGOs under the mandatory audit jurisdiction of the CAG to prevent the privatisation of profits and
                       socialisation of losses.
 Transitioning to    y Shift from sample-based, post-mortem auditing to 100% data-driven auditing using Big Data,
 Real-Time             Artificial Intelligence, and the integration of government financial portals (like PFMS) to flag
 Digital Auditing      financial anomalies in real-time.

In an era characterised by complex public-private financial architectures and massive welfare expenditures, the CAG can
no longer be restricted to the role of a traditional accountant conducting post-mortem compliance checks. To preserve
the sanctity of legislative control over the executive, the CAG must be statutorily empowered to audit PPPs, modernised
with real-time data analytics, and shielded by a collegium-based appointment, ensuring it remains the ultimate bulwark
of India's financial integrity.
pyq: “The duty of the Comptroller and Auditor General is not merely to ensure the legality of expenditure but also its propriety.” Comment. (2024)

“The Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) has a very vital role to play.” Explain how this is reflected in the method and terms of his appointment as well as the range of powers he can exercise. (2018)

Exercise of CAG’s powers in relation to the accounts of the Union and the States is derived from Article 149 of the Indian Constitution. Discuss whether audit of the Government’s policy implementation could amount to overstepping its own (CAG) jurisdiction (2016)

chapters.part: Constitutional Bodies
chapters.text:
31                 Finance Commission

Enshrined under Article 280 of the Constitution, the Finance Commission serves as the supreme Constitutional architect of fiscal
federalism in India. Designed to correct the inherent vertical and horizontal imbalances of a 'holding-together' federation, the
Commission acts as the balancing wheel, ensuring that the political autonomy of the States is not suffocated by financial dependency
on the Union.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.     How have the recommendations of the 14th Finance Commission of India enabled the
            States to improve their fiscal position? (2021)
     2.     How is the Finance Commission of India constituted? What do you know about the
            terms of reference of the recently constituted Finance Commission? Discuss. (2018)


31.1 The Core Framework and Responsibilities
   Constitutional                           Description                                          Significance
     Provision
 Article 280              Comprises a Chairman and 4 members.                   Ensures the body has a blend of judicial
 (Composition)            Parliament determines their qualifications            impartiality, macroeconomic expertise, and
                          (usually experts in public affairs, economics,        administrative foresight.
                          and high court judges).
 Article 280(3)          1. Tax Devolution: Distribution of net tax             The Evolution: Following the 73rd and 74th
 (The Core Duties)          proceeds between Centre and States (Vertical)       Amendments, the FC's role expanded massively
                            and among the States (Horizontal).                  from merely balancing Centre-State relations to
                         2. Grants-in-Aid (Art 275): Principles governing       empowering grassroots democratic institutions
                            grants to states in need.                           (Local bodies).
                         3. Local Body Augmentation: Measures to
                            supplement state resources for Panchayats/
                            Municipalities.
 Article 281              The President shall cause every recommendation        Advisory but Authoritative: The
 (Recommendations)        made by the Finance Commission to be laid             recommendations are technically advisory, not
                          before each House of Parliament, along with an        legally binding on the government. However, by
                          explanatory memorandum on the action taken.           convention, the formula for tax devolution is
                                                                                universally accepted to prevent federal collapse.

31.2 16th Finance Commission Recommendations
   Dimensions                           Recommendations                                                Impact
 Vertical              Retained at 41% of the divisible pool (unchanged from the      The "Grand Bargain" Debate: States
 Devolution            15th FC).                                                      demanded an increase to 50% due to the
                                                                                      shrinking divisible pool caused by the
                                                                                      Centre's heavy reliance on cesses and
                                                                                      surcharges. The FC opted for stability over
                                                                                      an increase.
 Grants-in-Aid         y Massive Shift: The 16th FC entirely discontinued             y Ends the era of "bailout" grants. States
                         Revenue Deficit Grants, Sector-specific, and State-            with poor fiscal management will no
                         specific grants.                                               longer be cushioned by Revenue Deficit
                       y Funds are now strictly routed to Local Bodies and              grants, forcing them to increase their
                         Disaster Management.                                           own-source revenues.


Urbanization &      Urban Local Body (ULB) share increased to 45% of all          Acknowledges India's rapid demographic
Local Bodies        local grants. Introduced an Urbanisation Premium (for         shift (projected 41% urbanization by 2031)
                    merging peri-urban villages) and tied grants to wastewater    and forces states to proactively manage
                    management.                                                   rural-to-urban transitions rather than
                                                                                  reacting to slum formations.
Fiscal Discipline   y Rationalise Freebies: It strongly urged states to review    y A direct strike against the populist
& Subsidies           and rationalise their subsidy expenditure.                    "freebie culture." By forcing off-budget
                    y Strict Debt Rules: States capped at 3% fiscal deficit.        borrowings onto the main ledger, it
                      Ordered a complete halt to Off-Budget Borrowings,             prevents states from masking their true
                      bringing all hidden debts onto official books.                debt-to-GDP ratios.


31.3 Challenges
    Challenge                                                       Description
The North-South        y The Issue: Since the 15th FC (and continuing into the 16th FC debates), the Commission uses the
Divide                   2011 Census (instead of 1971) to allocate funds.
(The Demographic       y The Friction: Southern states (like TN and Kerala) argue this heavily penalises them for
Penalty)                 successfully stabilising their populations, while rewarding Northern states (like UP and Bihar) for
                         demographic failure, threatening the "cooperative" spirit of federalism.
The "Cess and          y The Issue: Under Article 271, Cesses and Surcharges levied by the Centre do not form part of the
Surcharge"               divisible pool (states get no share).
Loophole               y The Friction: The Centre has aggressively increased cesses (from roughly 10% of gross tax
(Article 271)            revenue a decade ago to over 20% today). This artificially shrinks the pie available for states,
                         rendering the FC's high devolution percentages (e.g., 41%) practically much lower on the ground.
Conditionality of      y The Issue: The FC increasingly ties Grants-in-aid to specific sectoral reforms (e.g., power sector
Grants                   reforms or "One Nation One Ration Card").
                       y The Friction: States view this "performance-based" funding as a backdoor encroachment by the
                         Union into State Subjects, undermining their fiscal autonomy.
Lack of                y The Issue: Being a temporary body constituted every 5 years, the FC lacks a permanent
Institutional            secretariat.
Continuity             y Once a Commission submits its report, it is dissolved, leaving no institutional mechanism to
                         monitor whether the Centre actually releases the recommended funds or compliance.


31.4 Way Forward
     Reform Area                                                 Recommendations
 Capping the Cess &        Reform: Amend the Constitution or establish a binding statutory ceiling to cap the total collection
 Surcharge Levy            of cesses and surcharges at a maximum of 10% of the Union’s Gross Tax Revenue. Anything
                           collected beyond this must be mandatorily routed into the divisible pool.
 Rewarding                 Reform: To appease progressive states and neutralise the "demographic penalty", the 16th Finance
 Performance               Commission must assign significantly higher weightage to criteria like Demographic Performance,
 (Weightage)               Tax Effort, and Ecological Stewardship (forest cover), shifting the focus from purely need-based
                           equalisation to efficiency-based incentivisation.
 Permanent                 Reform: Upgrade the Finance Commission into a permanent body with a standing secretariat. This
 Secretariat for the       will ensure institutional memory, allow for real-time monitoring of fund utilisation by states, and
 FC                        provide continuous macroeconomic advisory.
 Rationalizing             y Reform: The Centre often bypasses the FC by using Article 282 (Discretionary Grants) for
 Discretionary Grants        massive centrally sponsored schemes.
                           y The mandate of the FC should be expanded to review and rationalise all discretionary transfers to
                             ensure they do not undermine the statutory devolution formula.
As India navigates widening economic disparities and aggressive demographic transitions, the Finance Commission can no
longer operate merely as a quinquennial accounting body. To safeguard the structural integrity of the Republic, it must transform
into a permanent institution of fiscal equity - one that surgically plugs the 'cess and surcharge' loopholes, rewards progressive
governance, and ensures that the pursuit of national equalisation does not inadvertently penalise the very states driving India's
economic engine.
pyq: How have the recommendations of the 14th Finance Commission of India enabled the States to improve their fiscal position? (2021)
How is the Finance Commission of India constituted? What do you know about the terms of reference of the recently constituted Finance Commission? Discuss. (2018)

chapters.t: Attorney General of India and Advocate General of State
chapters.part: Constitutional Bodies
chapters.text:
32                Attorney General of India and
                       Advocate General of State
Enshrined under Article 76 and Article 165 of the Constitution, the Attorney General and the Advocate General serve as the
primary legal conduits between the Executive and the Judiciary. They are entrusted with a unique dual mandate: to fiercely
defend the policies of the elected government while simultaneously upholding the absolute supremacy of constitutional morality
as impartial officers of the court.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    The Attorney General of India plays a crucial role in guiding the legal framework of the
            Union Government and ensuring sound governance through legal counsel.” Discuss his
            responsibilities, rights and limitations in this regard. (2025)
      2.    “The Attorney-General is the chief legal adviser and lawyer of the Government of India.”
            Discuss (2019)


32.1 Constitutional Architecture

  Constitutional           Attorney General               Advocate General                           Significance
    Aspect                  of India (AGI)                 of State (AGS)
 Constitutional          Article 76                     Article 165                     Created to ensure the government acts
 Mandate                                                                                strictly within legal bounds.
 Qualifications          Must be qualified to be a      Must be qualified to be a       Ensures the highest calibre of legal
                         Supreme Court Judge (5         High Court Judge (10 yrs        expertise equivalent to the apex
                         yrs HC Judge / 10 yrs HC       Judicial Office / 10 yrs HC     judiciary.
                         Advocate / Eminent             Advocate).
                         Jurist).
 Appointment             By the President (on           By the Governor (on advice      Structurally, they are political
                         advice of the Union            of the State Cabinet).          appointees, not career bureaucrats
                         Cabinet).
 Tenure & Removal        No fixed tenure. Holds         No fixed tenure. Holds          The Vulnerability: They lack the
                         office during the              office during the "Pleasure     security of tenure enjoyed by the ECI or
                         "Pleasure of the               of the Governor."               CAG, making them subservient to
                         President."                                                    executive whims.
 Resignation             Often resigns following a      Often resigns following a       Reinforces the reality that the office is
                         change in government.          change in government.           co-terminus with the political executive.

32.2 Roles of Attorney General
  Functional Domain                                                         Details
 Primary Advisory Role        Legal Advisor: Advises the Union on complex matters (e.g., GST, amendments).
                              President's Assignments: Drafts legal documents and renders opinions.
 Representation of the        Court Advocate: Defends the Centre in SC/HCs (e.g., Aadhaar, CAA, NRC).
 Union                        Art. 143: Represents the Govt. in Presidential advisory references to the SC.
 Constitutional &             Interpretation: Assists courts in clarifying constitutional laws (e.g., Privacy case).
 Parliamentary                Art. 88: Participates in Parliament (no vote) to aid complex law-making.
 Strategic &                  International Guidance: Advises on global treaties (like Paris Climate Agreement).
 Reformative                  Legal Reforms: Suggests improvements to existing laws and judicial processes.


32.3 Powers and Limitations
                              Rights                                                            Limitations
 Right of Audience: The AGI has the right of audience in all            No Anti-Government Advice: Cannot appear against the
 courts in the territory of India. (AGS has it before any court         Government without permission.
 within the State).
 Legislative Privileges (Art 88 / Art 177): They have the right         No Criminal Defense: Cannot defend accused persons in
 to speak and take part in the proceedings of both Houses of            criminal prosecutions without the explicit permission of the
 Parliament/State Legislature or any joint sitting/committee.           Government.
 Immunity: Enjoys all the privileges and immunities available           Corporate Board Bar: Cannot accept appointments as a
 to an MP/MLA, despite not being a member of the House.                 director in any company or corporation without Government
                                                                        permission.
 The Private Practice Loophole: They are not full-time                  The Friction: This private practice allowance is heavily
 government servants. They are allowed to continue their                criticised for creating conflicts of interest between private
 private legal practice.                                                corporate clients and public duty.

32.4 Challenges
       Challenge                                                           Description
 The "Pleasure                 The Issue: Because the AGI/AGS can be dismissed instantly without cause, they often function as
 Doctrine" &                   political mouthpieces rather than independent legal advisors.
 Partisanship                  Impact: They are sometimes forced to defend clearly unconstitutional laws or executive overreach
                               in the Supreme Court to appease the ruling party.
 Conflict of Interest          The Issue: Allowing the top government lawyer to maintain a private practice creates massive
 (Private Practice)            ethical grey areas.
                               Impact: An AGI might represent the government on Monday and represent a corporate giant on
                               Tuesday. Even with safeguards, the sheer access to classified government policy creates an
                               unparalleled advantage in private practice.
 Shielding the                 The Issue: Governments increasingly use the AGI/AGS to file "sealed cover" reports in courts,
 Executive from                shielding controversial executive decisions (like electoral bonds or security procurements) from
 Scrutiny                      public transparency and adversarial judicial scrutiny.

32.5 Way Forward
         Aspect                                                            Description
 Statutory Security of        y Implement a fixed statutory tenure (e.g., 3 or 5 years) that survives changes in government,
 Tenure                         removing the Damocles sword of the "Pleasure Doctrine".
 Banning Private              y The Law Commission has debated whether the AGI/AGS should be made full-time
 Practice                       government servants. Prohibiting private practice entirely during their tenure would eliminate
                                all corporate conflicts of interest.
 The UK Model                 y In the UK, the Attorney General is a political figure (Cabinet Minister), but the actual
 (Separating Politics           prosecutions are handled by an independent Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP).
 from Prosecution)            y India should establish an independent DPP with constitutional backing to handle criminal
                                matters neutrally, leaving the AGI to handle constitutional advice.
 Enhancing                    y Limit the AGI's use of "sealed cover jurisprudence". The office must prioritise its duty as an
 Transparency                   officer of the court over its duty as a defender of the executive by advising the government to
                                drop unconstitutional bills before they become law.
The Attorney General and Advocate General serve as the indispensable "first law officers" to the Union and State executives respectively,
providing vital constitutional continuity and legal counsel across both tiers of governance. Operating under a fusion-of-powers model,
they act as the essential bridge between the political executive and the judiciary by defending state interests and assisting legislatures
on complex legal questions.
pyq: The Attorney General of India plays a crucial role in guiding the legal framework of the Union Government and ensuring sound governance through legal counsel.” Discuss his responsibilities, rights and limitations in this regard. (2025)
“The Attorney-General is the chief legal adviser and lawyer of the Government of India.” Discuss (2019)

chapters.t: Social Justice Commissions: NCSC, NCST, & NCBC
chapters.part: Constitutional Bodies
chapters.text:
33                 Social Justice Commissions:
                        NCSC, NCST, & NCBC
Enshrined in Part XVI of the Constitution, the National Commissions for SCs, STs, and Backward Classes represent the institutional
bulwark of India's affirmative action framework. Evolving from a single Special Officer to a triad of robust constitutional bodies,
they are mandated to oversee the socio-economic empowerment of marginalized communities, ensuring that the constitutional promise
of equality is translated from legal text to lived reality.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Discuss the role of the National Commission for Backward Classes in the wake of its transformation from a
            statutory body to a constitutional body. (2022)

33.1 Constitutional Evolution & Architecture
  Commission                  Constitutional                   Composition &                        Significance
                            Evolution & Article                Appointment
 NCSC                 Article 338: Originally, there was      1 Chairman, 1           The foundational body.
 (Scheduled           only one Special Officer. The 65th      Vice-Chairman, 3        Mandated to investigate specific
 Castes)              Amendment (1990) created a              Members.                complaints regarding the deprivation of
                      combined multi-member                   Appointed by the        rights of the scheduled castes.
                      commission for both SCs and STs.        President (warrant
                                                              under hand and seal).
 NCST                 Article 338A: The 89th                  Same as NCSC            The Tribal Nuance: Created because
 (Scheduled           Amendment (2003) bifurcated the         (1+1+3).                tribal issues (forest rights, displacement,
 Tribes)              combined commission, creating a         Appointed by the        PESA) are geographically and culturally
                      separate NCST to focus                  President.              distinct from the untouchability issues
                      exclusively on tribal nuances.                                  faced by SCs.
 NCBC                 Article 338B: Previously a mere         Same as NCSC            The Parity Shift: Brought the NCBC on
 (Backward            statutory body under an Act of          (1+1+3).                par with the NCSC/NCST, giving it the
 Classes)             1993. Elevated to constitutional        Appointed by the        power to hear specific grievances of
                      status by the 102nd Amendment           President.              OBCs, rather than just advising on
                      (2018).                                                         inclusion/exclusion in the central list.

33.2 Core Powers (The Civil Court Authority)
All three Commissions share the exact same investigative powers under the Constitution.
 Powers of a Civil Court: When investigating any matter or enquiring into any complaint, they have the powers of a civil court
     trying a suit, specifically regarding:
            Summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person and examining them on oath.
            Requiring the discovery and production of any document.
            Receiving evidence on affidavits.
            Requisitioning any public record from any court or office.
 Mandatory Consultation: The Union and every State Government shall consult the respective Commission on all major
     policy matters affecting SCs, STs, or SEBCs (Socially and Educationally Backward Classes).

33.3 Challenges
     Challenge                                                           Details
 The "Toothless          y The Issue: Despite having the powers of a civil court to summon, they have absolutely no power to
 Tiger" Critique           penalise. Their reports are purely recommendatory. Governments routinely ignore their findings, and
                           Action Taken Reports (ATRs) are delayed for years in Parliament.
 The Sub-                y The Issue: The most burning contemporary debate. The Commissions are struggling to address
 Categorization            "elite capture" within reservations.
 Dilemma                 y Context: Demands from the Madiga community (NCSC) and the findings of the Justice Rohini
                           Commission (NCBC) point to the need to sub-categorise quotas so that the most marginalised
                           within the SC/OBC categories get their due, challenging the Commissions to redefine
                           backwardness.


 The NCBC &             y The Issue: The 102nd Amendment initially caused chaos by implying states lost the power to
 Federal Friction         identify their own backward classes.
 (105th                 y The Fix: Parliament had to pass the 105th Amendment (2021) to clarify and restore the power of
 Amendment)               State Governments to maintain their own SEBC lists, showcasing a friction between central
                          oversight and federal autonomy.
 Vacancies and          y The Issue: These commissions frequently operate without a full quorum. Furthermore, they lack an
 Bureaucratic             independent investigative cadre (relying entirely on state police to investigate atrocities), leading to
 Apathy                   severe delays and compromised fact-finding in various cases.

33.4 Way Forward
    Reform Area                                                         Strategy
 Statutory Teeth for      Amend the respective constitutional articles to mandate that the Executive must table the Action
 Recommendations          Taken Report (ATR) in Parliament within a strictly fixed timeframe of six months. Unreasonable
                          rejection of recommendations should be subject to judicial review.
 Independent              Establish a dedicated, permanent investigative wing for all three commissions, drawing officers on
 Investigative Cadre      deputation who are structurally insulated from state-level political pressure, ensuring unbiased probes
                          into atrocities (SC/ST PoA Act violations).
 Proactive Policy         Shift focus from reactive grievance redressal to proactive policy design. The commissions must
 Formulation              aggressively audit the implementation of Sub-Plans (SCSP/TSP) and utilise big data to identify
                          micro-communities facing extreme intersectional vulnerability.
 Convergence and          Address jurisdictional overlaps (e.g., a tribal woman facing custodial violence falls under NCST,
 Joint Sittings           NCW, and NHRC) by institutionalising joint task forces to prevent bureaucratic ping-pong and
                          ensure swift justice for intersectional victims.

The constitutional elevation of the NCSC, NCST, and NCBC signifies India's enduring commitment to restorative justice.
However, a constitutional tag without punitive teeth renders these commissions as mere spectators to systemic discrimination.
To truly safeguard the marginalised, Parliament must empower these bodies with binding investigative authority and independent
administrative machinery, transforming them from passive advisory boards into formidable engines of socio-economic
egalitarianism.


Statutory, Regulatory
     and Various
Quasi-Judicial Bodies
pyq: Discuss the role of the National Commission for Backward Classes in the wake of its transformation from a statutory body to a constitutional body. (2022)

chapters.part: Statutory, Regulatory and Various Quasi-Judicial Bodies
chapters.text:
34                  National Human Rights
                         Commission
Established on 12th October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act (PHRA) and inspired by the Paris Principles (1991/1993),
the NHRC serves as India’s principal statutory guardian of life, liberty, equality, and dignity. However, as former Chairperson Justice
H.L. Dattu aptly observed, the NHRC is largely a "moral compass". Because its enforcement powers are constrained, its true efficacy
is realised only when it acts as an aggressive complement to the Judiciary, civil society, and other accountability mechanisms to
promote SDG 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions).

34.1 How Does the NHRC Use its Mandate to Support and Reduce the Burden on
      Constitutional Courts and Other Statutory Bodies?
   Function                Framework                                          Complements Judiciary
 Inquiry &          Suo motu or petition-based       Complementing Courts: Reduces judicial pendency by doing ground-level
 Investigation      inquiries equipped with          fact-finding.
                    Civil Court powers (CPC          Example: Issued notice to Odisha over delays in custodial death
                    1908). Upholds Article 21.       compensation, aiding the enforcement of the SC's D.K. Basu guidelines.
 Judicial           Can intervene in ongoing         Complementing Courts: Acts as an amicus curiae (friend of the court).
 Intervention       human rights proceedings         Example: The NHRC relies on the compensatory jurisprudence established
                    with the prior approval of       in the Nilabati Behera case to investigate custodial deaths and human rights
                    the court.                       violations, subsequently recommending that State governments pay
                                                     monetary relief to the victims' families.
 Institutional      Mandated by Protection of        Complementing State Machinery:
 Oversight          Human Rights Act (PHRA)          Issued notice to the UP government over the reported deaths of three
                    to visit jails, detention        prisoners in Varanasi jails. Its 1998 Jail Reform Guidelines complement the
                    centres, and state hospitals     SC's R.D. Upadhyay (2006) judgement on prison reforms.
                    to assess living conditions.
 Policy             Reviews safeguards under         Complementing Statutory Commissions:
 Review &           international covenants          Advocated for the Idate Commission Report (2017) for Denotified Tribes
 Advocacy           (ICCPR, ICESCR) and              (DNTs).
                    recommends policy shifts.        Note: NHRC coordinates with NCST, whose chief is an ex-officio member to
                                                     prevent overlapping jurisdictions.

34.2 Synergies with Accountability Mechanisms
  Institutional Partner            How the NHRC Complements Them                                 Landmark Examples
 The Judiciary                   Fact-Finding vs. Adjudication: Courts are              The Gujarat Riots (2002): The NHRC
 (Supreme/High Courts)           burdened with pendency and strict procedural           intervened in the Supreme Court, leading
                                 laws. The NHRC complements them by                     to the transfer of key trial cases outside the
                                 conducting rapid, on-ground fact-finding               state.
                                 missions. It often approaches the Supreme Court        Encounter Killings: SC mandated that all
                                 to enforce its findings when the government            encounter killings must be reported to the
                                 ignores them.                                          NHRC within 48 hours for independent
                                                                                        review.

 Other Statutory                 Intersectional Justice via Ex-Officio                  Custodial Violence: If a tribal youth dies
 Commissions                     Members: Human rights violations are rarely            in custody, the NCST and NHRC can pool
 (NCSC, NCST, NCW)               one-dimensional (e.g., violence against a Dalit        their investigative resources to establish a
                                 woman involves gender and caste). The PHRA             systemic pattern of abuse.
                                 mandates that the Chairpersons of the NCSC,
                                 NCST, NCW, and Minorities Commission sit as
                                 ex-officio members of the NHRC, ensuring a
                                 coordinated, intersectional approach rather than
                                 bureaucratic turf wars.
 Civil Society & NGOs            Institutionalizing Activism: NGOs have                 Regular interactions with human rights
                                 grassroots intelligence but lack official power. The   defenders to monitor illegal mining,
                                 NHRC acts as a bridge, utilizing NGOs as its eyes      bonded labor, and manual scavenging.
                                 and ears to bypass apathetic state police networks.


34.3 Recent NHRC Initiatives
      Area                     Initiative                                               Details
 Women &                Foxconn Case &                 Ordered a reinvestigation into gender bias in hiring (Foxconn); held
 Labor Rights           National Symposium             symposiums on workplace safety, female labour force participation, and
                                                       gig worker rights.
 Vulnerable             Comprehensive                  Issued specific directives on widows' rights, anti-begging (integration with
 Groups                 Advisories (2023-24)           the SMILE scheme), mental health, and child protection (CSAM).
 Transgender            National Conference            Evaluated 'Garima Greh' shelters to improve infrastructure; issued
 Rights                 (2025) & Policy                advisories for comprehensive welfare and livelihood support.
 Justice &              Relief for Custodial           Recommended ₹4.5 cr compensation in 89 cases; held a national
 Custodial Safety       Deaths & Prison Reforms        conference advocating the transformation of prisons into reform spaces.
 Social                 Anti-Trafficking Push          Directed states to appoint senior nodal officers explicitly to combat human
 Protection                                            trafficking after alarming state-level reports.
 Grievance              Aggressive Case                Handled over 73,849 complaints (including 108 suo motu cases),
 Redressal              Resolution (2024-25)           recommending around ₹9 crore in monetary relief to victims in 210 cases.
 Outreach &             Camp Sittings & Public         Held regional sittings (e.g., Guwahati, Shimla) to fast-track pending
 Fast-tracking          Hearings                       human rights cases and engage local police/NGOs directly.
 Awareness &            Competitions &                 Organised awareness contests (short films/photography on LGBTQ+ and
 Education              Specialized Training           caste); conducted human rights training in sensitive regions like Manipur.

Issues and Way Forward with the Functioning of NHRC
        Issue                   Evidence (GANHRI SCA, 2025–26)                                     Way Forward
 Conflict of Interest      NHRC relies on government-seconded police                Amend PHRA, 1993 to create an independent
 in Investigations         officers to investigate custodial torture and police     investigative wing with forensic, legal and
                           excesses; concern repeatedly flagged since 2017.         human rights experts.
 Executive Influence       The Secretary General is a government-deputed            Allow NHRC to independently appoint its
 in Administration         civil servant, undermining NHRC's independence.          Secretary General and senior officials.
 Lack of Diversity &       2 of 6 Commissioner posts vacant; only 93 women          Ensure time-bound appointments and adopt
 Vacancies                 out of 339 staff; inadequate representation of SC/       transparent diversity benchmarks in
                           STs, minorities, PwDs and LGBTQ+ persons.                appointments.
 Opaque                    Selection process remains executive-dominated,           Broaden the selection committee to include
 Appointment               limiting institutional autonomy.                         judiciary and civil society with transparent,
 Process                                                                            merit-based selection.
 Weak Action on            Limited action on custodial deaths, shrinking civic      Publish periodic thematic reports,
 Systemic Violations       space, Human rights defenders (HRDs),                    institutionalise civil society consultations, and
                           surveillance concerns; most cases end with               recommend prosecution & disciplinary action
                           compensation rather than prosecution.                    in serious violations.
 Institutional             NHRC questioned the Paris Principles/GANHRI              Implement GANHRI recommendations and
 Credibility               accreditation instead of addressing deficiencies.        strengthen compliance to restore global
 Concerns                                                                           credibility.
The NHRC remains the cornerstone of India's human rights protection architecture. Strengthening its independence,
enforcement powers, and institutional capacity is essential to transform it from a recommendatory body into an effective
guardian of constitutional rights.

chapters.part: Statutory, Regulatory and Various Quasi-Judicial Bodies
chapters.text:
35                  Central Vigilance
                         Commission
The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) is the apex statutory vigilance institution of the Government of India, mandated to combat
corruption and ensure integrity in public administration.
 Origin: It was established in 1964 via an executive resolution based on the recommendations of the Santhanam Committee
     on Prevention of Corruption (1962–64).
 Statutory Status: Originally an advisory body, it was granted statutory status by Parliament through the Central Vigilance
     Commission Act, 2003, following the Supreme Court’s directives in the landmark Vineet Narain case (1997).
 Nature: It is an independent body, free of control from any executive authority, responsible only to Parliament.

35.1 Structure and Composition
    Aspect                                                             Details
 Composition        Multi-member body: 1 Central Vigilance Commissioner (Chairperson) + Maximum of 2 Vigilance
                    Commissioners.
 Appointment        Appointed by the President on the recommendation of a high-powered committee comprising:
                    1. Prime Minister (Chairperson)
                    2. Union Minister of Home Affairs
                    3. Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha
 Tenure             4 years or until they attain the age of 65 years, whichever is earlier. They are not eligible for reappointment
                    in any Central/State government agency, ensuring post-retirement neutrality.
 Removal            Removed by the President on grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity only after an inquiry by the
                    Supreme Court. The President can also remove them for insolvency, paid outside employment, or moral
                    turpitude.
 Independence       Their salary, allowances, and service conditions are identical to those of the UPSC Chairman and Members,
                    charged to the Consolidated Fund of India.

35.2 Issues and Challenges
          Issue                                                Challenges & Data Points
 1. Massive Pendency      y Trial Delays: 7,072 corruption cases pending trial (379 pending for >20 years; 2,660 for >10 years).
     in Trials &           y Appellate Bottlenecks: 13,100 appeals/revisions pending in High Courts and the Supreme Court (606
     Appeals                 pending for >20 years).
 2. Delays in             y Unmet Timelines: While investigations should ideally conclude within 1 year, 529 corruption
     Investigation by        cases are pending investigation (56 for >5 years).
     CBI                   y Complex Scrutiny: Prolonged time required to scrutinise voluminous records, especially in
                             economic offences and bank frauds.
                           y Logistical Hurdles: Delays in locating and examining witnesses in remote areas.
 3. Administrative &      y Prosecution Sanction: Delays by competent authorities in granting sanction to prosecute corrupt
     Procedural              officials act as a major shield for the corrupt.
     Bottlenecks           y Letters Rogatory (LRs): Extreme delays in obtaining international cooperation and responses to
                             LRs in cross-border corruption cases.
 4. Manpower              y Resource Crunch: The report explicitly cites "inadequate manpower" and "excessive work" as
     and Capacity            primary reasons for delayed investigations.
     Constraints           y CVO Dependency: CVC heavily relies on the Chief Vigilance Officers (CVOs) of respective
                             departments, who often face a conflict of interest or lack adequate staff.
 5. Declining             y Dip in Success: The conviction rate in corruption cases dropped to 69.14% in 2024 from 71.47%
     Conviction              in 2023, indicating potential gaps in evidence gathering or prolonged trials weakening cases over
     Rates                   time (witnesses turning hostile/dying).
 6. "Toothless Tiger"     y Lack of Punitive Power: The CVC is fundamentally an advisory body. Central Government
     (Advisory Nature)       departments are free to either accept or reject its advice regarding disciplinary actions against
                             corrupt officials.
                           y It does not have the power to register criminal cases; it only exercises "superintendence" over the
                             CBI for Prevention of Corruption Act (PCA) cases.


 7. Lack of an              y Borrowed Personnel: The CVC lacks its own dedicated investigative machinery. It is entirely
     Independent               dependent on the CBI or departmental CVOs for field investigations, limiting its direct
     Investigation Wing        operational control.
 8. Jurisdictional          y Limited Scope: The CVC's jurisdiction primarily covers Group 'A' officers of the Central
     Limitations               Government and higher-ups in PSUs/Public Sector Banks. It does not comprehensively cover all
                               government employees or state-level machinery.

35.3 Way Forward
  Category of Reform                                          Solutions & Recommendations
 1. Tackling Judicial           y Dedicated Fast-Track Courts: Establish special CBI courts mandated to conduct day-to-
     Pendency & Trial              day hearings without arbitrary adjournments.
     Delays                      y Strict Appellate Timeframes: Create specialised benches in High Courts and the Supreme
                                   Court with a statutory time limit (e.g., 6–12 months) for disposing of appeals under the
                                   Prevention of Corruption Act (PCA).
                                 y Digitisation of Evidence: Leverage the e-Courts project to digitise voluminous charge
                                   sheets and evidence to prevent delays caused by misplaced physical files.
 2. Reforming the               y Augmenting Expertise: Directly recruit subject-matter specialists (chartered accountants,
     Investigation Process         cyber forensics experts, financial analysts) to expedite the scrutiny of complex economic
     (CBI & CVOs)                  offences.
                                 y Enforcing Deadlines: Strictly monitor the CVC's 1-year investigation completion deadline;
                                   hold Investigating Officers (IOs) accountable for unjustified delays through performance
                                   reviews.
                                 y Streamlining Letters Rogatory (LRs): Establish a dedicated MEA-MHA cell to fast-track
                                   mutual legal assistance and LRs to prevent stalling in cross-border probes.
 3. Institutional               y From Advisory to Binding Authority: Amend the CVC Act so that if a Ministry rejects the
     Empowerment of the            CVC’s recommendation for disciplinary action, it must record the reasons in writing and
     CVC                           table them before Parliament.
                                 y Independent Investigative Wing: Provide the CVC with its own dedicated, independent
                                   investigative cadre to reduce its over-reliance on the CBI and departmental CVOs.
                                 y Protecting CVOs: Ensure CVO independence by mandating that their Annual Confidential
                                   Reports (ACRs) are written entirely by the CVC, shielding them from departmental
                                   pressure.
 4. Addressing                  y Enforcing 'Deemed Sanction': Strictly enforce the 3-month statutory limit for competent
     Administrative                authorities to grant prosecution sanction. If they fail to decide, it should be legally treated as
     Bottlenecks                   "deemed sanction" so trials can commence.
                                 y Protecting Honest Officers: Implement Hota Committee recommendations to protect honest
                                   civil servants from harassment for bona fide administrative errors, preventing 'policy
                                   paralysis'.
 5. Leveraging Technology       y AI for Document Scrutiny: Deploy Artificial Intelligence and Big Data analytics to scan
     & Preventive Vigilance        millions of pages of bank records and procurement documents to detect anomalies swiftly.
                                 y Minimising Human Interface: Expand the scope of e-Procurement, GeM (Government
                                   e-Marketplace), and Direct Benefit Transfers (DBT) to eliminate the root causes of systemic
                                   bribery.
To transform the CVC from an advisory "toothless tiger" into a formidable anti-corruption bulwark, it is imperative to grant
its recommendations binding statutory authority and equip it with an independent investigative cadre. Ultimately, coupling
this institutional empowerment with technological integration and fast-tracked judicial processes is vital to ensuring swift
accountability and upholding the highest standards of integrity in India's public administration.

chapters.part: Statutory, Regulatory and Various Quasi-Judicial Bodies
chapters.text:
36                Lokpal

The Lokpal is India's premier independent statutory anti-corruption ombudsman, established to enquire into allegations of
corruption against certain categories of high-ranking public servants.

36.1 Evolution and Statutory Framework
      Aspect                                                           Details
 Global Origin        The concept originated with the Ombudsman in Sweden (1809) and the term "Lokpal" was coined by
                      L.M. Singhvi in 1963.
 First                In 1966, the 1st Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC) (headed by Morarji Desai) recommended
 Recommendation       setting up a Lokpal at the Centre and Lokayuktas in States.
 Subsequent           Recommended by the National Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution (NCRWC,
 Endorsements         2002) and the 2nd ARC (2005) under Veerappa Moily.
 Legislative          Between 1968 and 2011, eight separate Lokpal Bills were introduced in Parliament but repeatedly failed
 Attempts             to pass due to political deadlocks.
 Final Momentum       The "India Against Corruption" movement led by Anna Hazare in 2011 forced the government to
                      fast-track the legislation.
 Statutory            The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013, was passed by Parliament and received Presidential assent on
 Backing              January 1, 2014.

36.2 Structure, Composition, and Tenure
       Feature                                                          Details
 Composition             Consists of a Chairperson (former CJI/SC Judge or eminent person) and a maximum of 8 members.
 Representation          50% must be judicial members. Furthermore, at least 50% of total members must be from SC, ST,
 Mandate                 OBC, minorities, or women.
 Selection               Comprises: 1. Prime Minister (Chair), 2. Speaker of Lok Sabha, 3. Leader of Opposition (LS), 4.
 Committee               Chief Justice of India (or SC judge nominee), 5. Eminent jurist nominated by the President.
 The LoP                 Due to the absence of a recognised LoP (requiring 10% of seats), the Act was amended to include the
 Amendment (2016)        Leader of the Single Largest Opposition Party in the Selection Committee.
 Search Committee        The Selection Committee must form a Search Committee of at least 7 persons to prepare a panel of
                         names. Note: The 50% reservation rule (SC/ST/OBC/Women) applies to this committee as well.
 Term & Conditions       5 years or until 70 years of age. Salaries and allowances are equivalent to the CJI (for the
                         Chairperson) and Supreme Court Judges (for members).
 Strict Post-Tenure      Members are barred from any diplomatic assignment, appointment to any territory/state as
 Bar                     Administrator/Governor, or contesting any election (President/VP/MP/MLA) for 5 years after
                         ceasing to hold office.

36.3 Jurisdiction and Operational Functions
       Aspect                                         Provisions, Scope, and Exemptions
 Public Servants        Prime Minister, Union Ministers, MPs, and Group A, B, C, and D officers of the Central Government.
 Covered
 NGOs and               Jurisdiction extends to anyone managing a society/trust/NGO that receives a government grant above
 Societies              ₹1 crore or foreign contributions above ₹10 lakh (under FCRA).
 PM Exceptions          Cannot enquire into allegations against the PM related to: International Relations, External/Internal
                        Security, Public Order, Atomic Energy, and Space.
 MPs' Privilege         Does not have jurisdiction over any speech made or vote given by an MP in Parliament (protected
 Exemption              under Article 105).


 Asset Confiscation        Vested with the power to provisionally attach and confiscate property, assets, and receipts acquired by
                           corrupt means, even during the pendency of prosecution (before conviction).
 Transfer of Officers      Can recommend the transfer or suspension of public servants connected with corruption allegations to
                           prevent evidence tampering.
 Limitation Period         The Lokpal cannot enquire into any complaint made after the expiry of 7 years from the date on which
                           the offense was committed.

36.4 Issues and Way Forward
                   Issues / Limitations                                                Suggested Reforms
 No Suo Motu Power: The Lokpal cannot initiate                    Grant Suo Motu Cognisance: Empower the Lokpal to take up
 enquiries on its own; it can only act on complaints filed        cases based on media reports or intelligence, similar to the
 in the prescribed format.                                        NHRC or Supreme Court.
 Exclusion of the Judiciary: The Lokpal Act completely            Judicial Standards Bill: Enact a separate, robust mechanism
 excludes the higher judiciary from its ambit.                    (like the Judicial Standards and Accountability Bill) to address
                                                                  judicial corruption.
 Lack of Independent Machinery: Relies heavily on the             Dedicated Investigation Cadre: Equip the Lokpal with its own
 CBI and CVC. The CBI continues to be administratively            independent investigative wing to free it from executive
 controlled by the DoPT (Executive).                              pressure.
 Anonymous Complaints Barred: The Act severely                    Operationalise Whistleblower Act: Fully implement the
 punishes "false and frivolous" complaints (up to 1-year          Whistle Blowers Protection Act, 2014, and protect the identity of
 imprisonment) and bans anonymous complaints,                     complainants to encourage reporting.
 deterring honest whistleblowers.
 Administrative Friction: Overlap of duties between               Institutional Synergy: Clearly define the hierarchy. Make the
 CVC (handling vigilance for Group C & D) and Lokpal.             CVC an explicit investigative arm and strengthen coordination
                                                                  between Lokpal and CVC.
The Lokpal represents the culmination of decades of efforts to create an independent and credible anti-corruption watchdog at the
national level. While the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 has strengthened accountability in public life, its effectiveness remains
constrained by institutional and operational limitations. Empowering the Lokpal with greater functional autonomy, independent
investigative capacity, and stronger whistleblower protection can transform it into a robust pillar of transparent, ethical, and citizen-
centric governance.

chapters.part: Statutory, Regulatory and Various Quasi-Judicial Bodies
chapters.text:
37                 Lokayukta

The Lokayukta is India's adaptation of the Swedish Ombudsman (established 1809; the word means "legal representative"). The First
Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC, 1966–70) under Morarji Desai, in its landmark report on "Problems of Redress of
Citizens' Grievances," first recommended independent authorities at both the Union (Lokpal) and state (Lokayukta) levels.
   Maharashtra (1971): First state to establish a Lokayukta under the Maharashtra Lokayukta and Upa-Lokayuktas Act,
    1971; widely regarded as India's first Lokayukta institution (Odisha's 1970 Lokpal used different nomenclature).
   Karnataka (1984): Introduced the most powerful Lokayukta model, featuring independent police powers, suo motu
    jurisdiction, and special courts; often considered the gold standard for anti-corruption ombudsman institutions.

     Feature                                      Standard Provisions & State Variations
 Appointment          Appointed by the Governor of the respective state.
 Consultation         Different states have different procedures. In most states, the Governor must consult the Chief Justice of
 Process              the State High Court and the Leader of Opposition (LoP) in the State Legislative Assembly.
 Qualifications       Generally restricted to individuals with a high judicial background (e.g., a former Supreme Court Judge
                      or a former Chief Justice of a High Court). Some states allow eminent non-judicial members.
 Tenure               Typically 5 years or until the age of 65/70.
                      Variation: States like Uttar Pradesh and Rajasthan previously had Lokayukta's tenure set to 8 years.
 Removal              Enjoys security of tenure. Cannot be dismissed or transferred by the state executive. Removal requires an
 Process              impeachment-style motion passed by the state legislature, ensuring independence.

Jurisdiction and Operational Powers
     Aspect                                          Provisions, Scope, and Exemptions
 The Chief            Included: States like Himachal Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra and Gujarat
 Minister             bring the CM under the Lokayukta's ambit.
                      Excluded: States like Uttar Pradesh and Rajasthan explicitly exempt the CM from its jurisdiction.
 Public Servants      Usually includes State Ministers, MLAs, members of local authorities, statutory bodies, and all state
 Covered              government officers.
 Investigative        Most Lokayuktas lack their own independent investigation wing. They must rely on state agencies like
 Mechanism            the Anti-Corruption Bureau (ACB) or the State CID, which creates a conflict of interest.
 Nature of            Their powers are predominantly advisory/recommendatory. After an inquiry, the Lokayukta submits an
 Action               annual consolidated report to the Governor.
 Legislative          The Governor is constitutionally bound to lay this report before the State Legislature, accompanied by an
 Accountability       explanatory memorandum detailing the government's action (or reasons for inaction) on the
                      recommendations. → This obligation arises from respective state Lokayukta Acts, not directly from the
                      Constitution.

Lokayukta remains one of India's most important instruments for promoting transparency, integrity, and citizen-centric
governance. Experiences from states such as Karnataka demonstrate that a well-designed and empowered Lokayukta can serve
as an effective check on corruption. With greater institutional independence, stronger investigative capacity, and uniform
standards across states, the institution can evolve into a cornerstone of accountable and ethical governance in India's federal
framework.
